instruction: How can clinicians adjust meropenem administration to improve treatment efficacy in a patient with suboptimal response?
steps: Perform mNGS on both blood and focal tissue specimens.
Determine meropenem susceptibility through pleural effusion susceptibility testing.
Monitor the patient’s body temperature and inflammatory markers to assess therapeutic response.
Assess PK/PD parameters, focusing on whether 100% fT > MIC is achieved with the current regimen.
If the patient’s response is suboptimal and PK/PD indicates insufficient T > MIC, switch meropenem infusion to an extended regimen.
Administer 2 g of meropenem over 3 hours every 8 hours.
Continue monitoring body temperature and inflammatory markers to confirm efficacy.
constraints: mNGS must be performed on both blood and focal tissue samples.
Meropenem susceptibility must be confirmed via susceptibility testing.
Infusion time must be 3 hours for the extended regimen.
Therapeutic response assessment must include body temperature and inflammatory markers.

instruction: How can we reconstruct metagenome-assembled genomes from short-read data and annotate their functional content?
steps: Assemble short reads from each sample using MEGAHIT (v1.2.9).
Bin the resulting contigs and optimize bins with MetaWRAP (v1.3.2) using MataBAT2, MaxBin2 and CONCOCT.
Retain only bins that meet quality thresholds of ≥70 % completeness, ≤5 % contamination and an ANI of ≥99.5 %.
Classify the high‑quality MAGs taxonomically with GTDB_tk (v2.4.0).
Calculate the relative abundance of each MAG in reads per kilobase per million mapped reads (RPKM) using Coverm (v0.6.1).
Align predicted proteins from the MAGs against mobileOG‑db, VFDB and Deep‑ARG (v1.0.2) using BLASTp (v0.9.22).
Label MAGs that contain at least one ARG as antibiotic‑resistant bacteria (ARB).
Label MAGs that contain at least one virulence factor (VF) as putative pathogenic bacteria.
constraints: Use MEGAHIT version 1.2.9 for assembly.
Use MetaWRAP version 1.3.2 with MataBAT2, MaxBin2 and CONCOCT for binning.
Apply MAG quality filters of ≥70 % completeness, ≤5 % contamination and ANI ≥99.5 %.
Perform taxonomic classification with GTDB_tk version 2.4.0.
Compute RPKM values with Coverm version 0.6.1.
Conduct protein alignments with BLASTp version 0.9.22 against mobileOG‑db, VFDB and Deep‑ARG v1.0.2.

instruction: How can volatile metabolites of Zaopei samples be extracted and analyzed by HS‑SPME and GC–MS?
steps: Weigh 4 g of Zaopei sample and mix with 10 mL physiological saline, incubate overnight at 4 °C.
Centrifuge the mixture to collect the supernatant.
Transfer 5.0 mL of the supernatant to a 20 mL headspace vial containing 1.5 g NaCl and 20 µL amyl acetate (69 mg L⁻¹) as internal standard.
Insert a 50/30 µm DVB/CAR/PDMS fiber into the vial and extract volatiles for 50 min at 45 °C.
Inject the extracted sample into a GC–MS (Agilent 6890 GC/5973 N) under the specified conditions (DB‑WAX column, helium 1.0 mL min⁻¹, temperature program, MS 70 eV, 35–400 amu).
Identify volatile compounds by comparing mass spectra to the NIST 17 GC–MS database and retention indices with NIST Chemistry WebBook.
Semi‑quantify volatile metabolites using the peak area ratio relative to the internal standard.
constraints: Use 4 g sample mixed with 10 mL physiological saline, incubated overnight at 4 °C.

instruction: How can tick samples be collected systematically from trail transects?
steps: Use a measuring wheel to calculate and mark the start of each 75‑m transect along the trail.
Place a flag at the start of the transect.
Measure 15 m sections, placing a flag at the end of each, until five 15‑m stops are marked.
Position the dragnet at the first flag and pull it to the next flag, inspecting the cloth for ticks.
Remove any ticks found with tweezers and place them into an Eppendorf tube containing 0.25–0.75 mL 95 % ethanol.
Label each tube with the transect letter (A–J) and stop number (1–5) and the tick count if multiple ticks are present.
At the beginning of each stop, record GPS coordinates, canopy cover with a densiometer, and temperature/humidity with a thermohygrometer.
constraints: All equipment listed in the materials section must be available (measuring wheel, ≥10 flags, drag cloth, ≥50 Eppendorf tubes with 50 mL 75 % ethanol, tweezers, plastic bags, blank data sheet, densiometer, thermohygrometer, GPS).
Transects must be 75 m long, divided into five 15 m stops, with a total sample area of 750 m.
Samples must be stored in 95 % ethanol to prevent DNA degradation.

instruction: How can I perform untargeted metabolite analysis of Daqu samples?
steps: Place 50 mg of wet Daqu sample into a 2 mL centrifuge tube and add a 6 mm diameter grinding bead.

Add 400 µL of extraction solution (methanol : water 4 : 1 v/v) containing 0.02 mg mL⁻¹ L‑2‑chlorophenylalanine and extract by grinding with a Wonbio‑96c frozen tissue grinder for 6 min at 10 °C, 50 Hz.
Perform low‑temperature ultrasonic extraction for 30 min at 5 °C, 40 kHz.
Incubate the mixture at –20 °C for 30 min, then centrifuge 15 min at 4 °C, 13 000 g and transfer the supernatant to an injection vial for LC‑MS/MS.
Set up a Thermo UHPLC‑Q Exactive HF‑X system with an ACQUITY HSS T3 column (100 mm × 2.1 mm i.d., 1.8 µm) and run at a flow rate of 0.40 mL min⁻¹ and column temperature 40 °C.
Use mobile phase A (0.1 % formic acid in water) and mobile phase B (0.1 % formic acid in acetonitrile:isopropanol:water 47.5 : 47.5 : 5 v/v) for the gradient.

Acquire data in both positive and negative ionization modes in a single LC‑MS/MS run, switching rapidly between modes, with source temperature 425 °C, sheath gas flow 50 arb, auxiliary gas flow 13 arb, and ion‑spray voltage ±3500 V, using full MS resolution 60 000 and MS/MS resolution 7500.
Apply Data Dependent Acquisition selecting the top 10 most intense precursor ions per full scan, with AGC target 3 × 10⁶, dynamic exclusion 12 s, and mass range 70–1050 m/z.
Prepare a pooled QC sample from equal volumes (20 µL) of each extract, analyze it before the first sample and after every 10 samples to monitor stability.

Process raw data with Progenesis QI software, perform peak picking, alignment, and normalization, discard false positives, retain features present in ≥80 % of samples, impute values below limit, sum‑normalize, exclude variables with RSD > 30 % in QC, and log10‑transform the matrix.

Identify metabolites by querying accurate parent ion mass (≤10 ppm) and MS/MS spectra against HMDB, MetLin, KEGG, and Majorbio databases, keeping matches with consensus score >30 that have both experimental (score >35) and theoretical (score >40) matches.

Determine differential metabolites using OPLS‑DA VIP ≥1.0 and Student’s t‑test p < 0.05, then map significant metabolites to KEGG pathways and perform enrichment analysis with Fisher’s exact test (p < 0.05).
constraints: Use 50 mg of wet sample and 400 µL extraction solution (methanol : water 4 : 1 v/v) with 0.02 mg mL⁻¹ internal standard.
Grind with Wonbio‑96c frozen tissue grinder 6 min at 10 °C, 50 Hz, then ultrasonic extraction 30 min at 5 °C, 40 kHz.

Flow rate 0.40 mL min⁻¹, column temperature 40 °C.

Identification against HMDB, MetLin, KEGG, Majorbio with consensus score >30, experimental score >35, theoretical score >40.

instruction: How can a lab‑scale anammox‑AnMBR be constructed and operated?
steps: Inoculate the reactor with sludge washed three times with 0.9 % NaCl solution.
Feed the reactor with synthetic wastewater adjusted to a pH of 7.2 ± 0.1.
Supply a continuous mixed gas stream of 95 % Argon and 5 % CO₂ at 50 mL min⁻¹ to the reactor.
Collect mixed‑liquid sludge samples at the end of each operating stage.
Pump the synthetic wastewater from a feeding tank into a buffer tank equipped with a water‑level controller.
Install a unidirectional double‑check valve between the buffer tank and the reactor to prevent backflow.
Drive the flow from the buffer tank to the reactor using hydraulic pressure.
Install a three‑way valve on the reactor effluent pipeline and connect an external pipe to a pressure transducer to monitor transmembrane pressure.
Seal the reactor side port with a rubber stopper.
Use a needle or probe to sample or monitor pH and dissolved oxygen levels through the sealed port.
Wrap the reactor with aluminum foil to inhibit phototrophic growth.
Stir the reactor with a magnetic stirrer set to 200 rpm to circulate sludge and scour the membrane surface.

Synthetic wastewater pH 7.2 ± 0.1
Mixed gas composition 95 % Argon, 5 % CO₂
Mixed gas flow rate 50 mL min⁻¹
Magnetic stirring speed 200 rpm
Wash sludge with 0.9 % NaCl solution

instruction: How can endophytic bacteria be isolated and identified from Vitis vinifera plantlets?

Purify the PCR product using HiAccuBead
Send the purified product for Sanger sequencing on an ABI 3130 Genetic Analyzer
Analyze the resulting sequence with BLAST at NCBI, assigning species identity for sequences with ≥99 % similarity
Construct a phylogenetic tree using the Neighbor‑Joining method in the NCBI BLAST Tree View tool

Use primers 27 F (5′‑AGA GTT TGA TCM TGG CTC AG‑3′) and 1492R (5′‑GGT TAC CTT GTT ACG ACT T‑3′) at 10 pmol each
PCR uses 2× Taq PCR Master Mix (Genetbio) and 1 µL of each primer

instruction: How can I extract and analyze volatile compounds from samples using GC/MS?
steps: Spike each sample with 200 µL of internal standard solution (methyl nonanoate 100 mg/L) and acidify by adding 15 drops of hydrochloric acid.
Perform the first extraction with 5 mL of dichloromethane.
Centrifuge the mixture at 5000 × g for 15 min at 4 °C to facilitate phase separation.
Perform a second extraction with 5 mL of dichloromethane.
Centrifuge again under the same conditions to separate the phases.
Dry the combined organic phase over anhydrous sodium sulfate.
Adjust the volume to 10 mL with dichloromethane.
Add 150 µL of a second internal standard (3‑octanol 100 mg/L).
Concentrate the sample under a nitrogen gas stream to a final volume of 500 µL.
Inject 1 µL of the concentrated extract into a GC/MS in splitless mode at an inlet temperature of 250 °C.
Separate the volatile compounds on a DB‑Wax polar capillary column (30 m × 0.32 mm i.d., 0.50 µm film thickness) using helium as the carrier gas at a constant flow of 1.3 mL/min.

Program the oven temperature: hold at 40 °C for 5 min, ramp at 10 °C/min to 71.5 °C, then at 18 °C/min to 111 °C, followed by a ramp of 6 °C/min to 150 °C, and finally at 20 °C/min to 240 °C held for 9 min, with a post‑run temperature of 50 °C.
Set the MS transfer line temperature to 250 °C, the ion source to 230 °C, and the quadrupole to 150 °C, and operate in electron impact ionization mode at 70 eV.
Acquire data in scan mode over an m/z range of 43 to 300 with a solvent delay of 7.2 min.
Identify compounds by comparing retention times with commercial standards and the NIST library.
Calculate relative peak areas by dividing each target compound’s area by the areas of the two internal standards.
Determine the percentage of aroma recovery using the formula: %Aroma Recovery = (A_exp – A_ctrl) / A_0 × 100.
constraints: Use 5 mL dichloromethane for each extraction.
Centrifuge samples at 5000 × g for 15 min at 4 °C.
Internal standards: methyl nonanoate 100 mg/L and 3‑octanol 100 mg/L.
Concentrate under nitrogen to 500 µL.
Inject 1 µL in splitless mode at 250 °C.
GC column: DB‑Wax, 30 m × 0.32 mm i.d., 0.50 µm film thickness.
Helium carrier gas flow: 1.3 mL/min.
Oven temperature program as specified.
MS settings: 250 °C transfer line, 230 °C ion source, 150 °C quadrupole, 70 eV EI, m/z 43–300, 7.2 min solvent delay.

instruction: How can we assess the ecological risk of heavy metal contamination in sampled soils?
steps: Measure the concentration of each potentially toxic element (As, Cd, Cr, Cu, Ni, Pb, Zn) in every soil sample.
Retrieve the geochemical background value (Bn) for each element from GEMAS European geochemical baselines.
Calculate the Geo‑accumulation Index (Igeo) for each element using Igeo = log2(Cn / (1.5 * Bn)).
Assign each Igeo value to its contamination class (Class 0 to Class 6) according to the defined thresholds.
Compute the contamination factor (CFi) for each element as CFi = Ci / Bin.
Multiply each CF_i by its toxic response coefficient (Ti) and sum the products to obtain the Improved Potential Ecological Risk Index (IPERI).
Classify the cumulative IPERI into risk categories (low, moderate, considerable, very high) based on the specified thresholds.
Determine the minimum (Cmin_i) and maximum (Cmax_i) observed concentrations for each element across all samples.
Calculate the Synthetic Potential Ecological Risk Index (SPERI) using SPERI = (1/n) * Σ_i [(Ci - Cmin_i)/(Cmax_i - Cmin_i)].
Integrate the Igeo, IPERI, and SPERI results to evaluate overall ecological risk for each soil sample.
constraints: Use the GEMAS European geochemical baselines for background values.
Compute Igeo with a logarithm base of 2.
Apply the toxicity response coefficients: Cd = 30, Pb = 5, Cu = 5, Cr = 2, Ni = 5, Zn = 1, As = 10.
Classify Igeo and IPERI according to the thresholds provided in the study.

instruction: How can silicon‑rich magnetic biochar (SBC) be synthesized via in‑situ co‑precipitation of magnetite onto rice straw?
steps: Weigh 20 g of ground, air‑dried rice straw sieved to 1 mm.
Immerse the straw in 400 mL of a mixed FeCl₃ and FeSO₄ solution with a Fe²⁺:Fe³⁺ molar ratio of 1:2 and Fe²⁺ concentration of 0.1 M.
Add 5 M NaOH solution to the mixture to adjust the pH to 11–12.
Stir the mixture for 1 hour at room temperature.
Cover the beaker with plastic wrap and allow the mixture to settle for 24 hours.
Filter the resulting material and dry it.
Mix the dried material with 5 % calcium silicate by weight.
Place the mixture in a muffle furnace and pyrolyze at 700 °C under anaerobic conditions.

instruction: How can we collect and process lake water samples for microbial analysis across the Yunnan-Guizhou Plateau lakes?
steps: Design a grid of sampling sites across each lake proportionally to its surface area and distribute 18 sites in Dianchi, 13 in Erhai, and 10 in Qilu.

Analyze total nitrogen, nitrate, nitrite, ammonia, and total phosphorus following GB3838‑2002 environmental quality standards.

instruction: How can I process a wastewater sample for next‑generation sequencing?
steps: Centrifuge 100 mL of the sample at 4300 × g for 10 min at 4 °C.
Discard the supernatant and resuspend the pellet in 1.5 mL nuclease‑free water.
Transfer 1 mL of the resuspended pellet to ZR Bashing‑Bead Lysis Tubes and store on ice.
Perform mechanical disruption with a tissue homogenizer at 4000 rpm for 30 s, repeat twice, keeping the tubes on ice between cycles and after the final cycle.
Centrifuge the resulting suspension at 10,000 × g for 10 min at 4 °C.
Collect 1 mL of the supernatant and isolate DNA with the ZymoBiomics DNA Microprep Kit, eluting into 100 µL nuclease‑free water.
Assess DNA quality with the Fragment Analyzer (DNF‑488 HS gDNA kit) and quantify concentration with the Qubit 3 Fluorometer (dsDNA HS assay kit).
Prepare a sequencing library using the Nextera XT kit on the Biomek FXP robot, inputting 10 ng of DNA; if less than 10 ng is available, use 5 µL as the maximum input.
Quality‑control and quantify the library with the DNF‑474 HS NGS Kit on the Fragment Analyzer and with the Qubit 3.0 dsDNA HS assay kit.
Adjust 5 µL of the library to 1 nM with nuclease‑free water and denature with 5 µL of 0.1 M NaOH.
Add 5 µL TRIS‑HCl and hybridization buffer to achieve a final loading concentration of 1.4 pM.
Sequence on a MiniSeq device with the MiniSeqRapid Kit using 100‑bp single‑read settings, targeting 20 million reads.
constraints: Store collected samples at −20 °C until analysis.
Use only nuclease‑free water for resuspension, dilution, and library preparation.
All centrifugation steps must be performed at 4 °C unless otherwise specified.
Mechanical disruption must be performed with a tissue homogenizer at 4000 rpm for 30 s, twice, with ice intervals.
DNA extraction must follow the ZymoBiomics DNA Microprep Kit protocol, eluting into 100 µL.
Library preparation must use the Nextera XT kit with a 10 ng DNA input; if input <10 ng, use a maximum of 5 µL.
Sequencing must be performed on a MiniSeq device with the MiniSeqRapid Kit, 100‑bp single‑read mode, and aim for 20 million reads.

instruction: How can we perform in vitro colonic fermentation with probiotic treatment using a single IBD patient fecal sample?

steps: Fill each 500 mL bioreactor with the fermentation medium containing 40 g VS L⁻¹ substrate.
Add 20 g L⁻¹ calcium carbonate to the medium to buffer the pH.
Prepare and inoculate each bioreactor with Saccharomyces cerevisiae at the designated concentration (1, 2, 4, 6, 8 g L⁻¹) or leave blank for the control.
Place all bioreactors in a thermoregulated water bath set to 37 °C and attach the integrated agitation system set to 100 rpm.
Run the fermentation continuously for 5 days.
Withdraw a sample from each bioreactor every 24 hours.
Process each collected sample with the established analytical methods.
constraints: Substrate concentration 40 g VS L⁻¹
Working volume 500 mL
Temperature 37 °C
Agitation 100 rpm
CaCO3 20 g L⁻¹
Inoculum concentrations 1–8 g L⁻¹
Experiment duration 5 days

instruction: How can anaerobic fermentation of pre‑treated sludge be conducted and monitored for short‑chain fatty acid production?
steps: Pre‑cultivate sludge in a nutrient medium containing glucose, urea, yeast extract, peptone, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, and sodium bicarbonate until organic substrates are depleted.
Subject the inoculated sludge to heat shock at 100 °C for 30 min.
Add 2‑bromoethanesulfonate to a final concentration of 5 g L⁻¹ to inhibit methanogenic activity.
Transfer the pre‑treated sludge into 250 mL serum bottles containing 200 mL working volume with a total carbon concentration of 500 mmol C L⁻¹.
Set the substrate‑to‑inoculum ratio (based on volatile solids) to 1:1.
Adjust the initial pH of each reactor to 7.0 ± 0.2 and add deionized water to reach 200 mL total liquid volume.
Flush nitrogen gas through the headspace for 2 min to remove oxygen, then seal each bottle with a rubber stopper.
Incubate the sealed bottles in a thermostatic shaker at 35 °C and 120 rpm under anaerobic conditions.
Collect samples every three days, withdraw 5 mL of supernatant, and measure pH and analyze short‑chain fatty acids.
Consider fermentation complete when short‑chain fatty acid concentrations stabilize.
Perform all experimental setups in triplicate, including a blank containing only inoculum.
constraints: Reactor volume must be 200 mL working volume in 250 mL serum bottles.
Incubation temperature 35 °C and shaking speed 120 rpm.
Nitrogen flush for 2 min, sealed with rubber stoppers.
Initial pH adjustment to 7.0 ± 0.2.
BES concentration 5 g L⁻¹.
Substrate‑to‑inoculum ratio 1:1 based on volatile solids.
Sampling interval 3 days, 5 mL supernatant.
Triplicate runs with a blank control.
Total carbon concentration 500 mmol C L⁻¹.

instruction: How can metagenomic libraries be prepared and sequenced from fecal DNA samples?
steps: Extract DNA from fecal samples.
Fragment the extracted DNA to approximately 350 base pairs using sonication.
Amplify the fragmented DNA by PCR.
Select and purify the PCR products based on size.
Quantify the library with Qubit® and real‑time PCR.
Analyze the library’s size distribution using a bioanalyzer.
Pool qualified libraries according to effective concentration and required data amount.
Sequence the pooled libraries on Illumina sequencing platforms.
constraints: Sonication must produce fragments of approximately 350 bp suitable for Illumina sequencing.
Library quantification must use Qubit® and real‑time PCR for accurate measurement.
Size distribution assessment must be performed with a bioanalyzer.
Sequencing must be performed on Illumina platforms.

instruction: How can Rangifer tarandus pegivirus be isolated from reindeer serum samples using cell culture?
steps: Prepare MDBK, SMMC-7721, THP-1, and RAW264.8 cells in their respective media with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin–streptomycin.
Maintain all cell cultures at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO₂.
Seed the cells into 24‑well plates at a density of 2 × 10⁵ cells per well.
Dilute RPgV‑positive serum 1:20 and inoculate the diluted serum onto each cell line.
Incubate the inoculated cells for 2 hours.
Remove the inoculum and replace it with fresh culture medium.
Perform blind passaging of the cultures for three consecutive passages, collecting the supernatant after each passage.
Detect virus in the collected supernatants by nested PCR.
constraints: Use 37 °C incubation and 5% CO₂ atmosphere.

instruction: How can we evaluate strain-level abundance estimation accuracy on simulated 4 E. coli metagenomes?
steps: Obtain the reference genomes of the four E. coli strains (E24377A, H10407, Sakai, UTI89) from NCBI.
Define the four abundance vectors (log, uniform, asym1, asym2) as specified in Table 2.

For each abundance vector and each desired coverage (10×, 20×, 50×), use ART to simulate paired‑end Illumina reads for each strain with the parameters `-ss MSv3 -p -l 250 -f IC -m 600 -s 150`, where IC equals total coverage times the strain’s proportion.
Mix the simulated reads from all four strains to create a metagenomic sample for that coverage and abundance vector.
Construct a custom StrainScan database using the four query genomes and run StrainScan in default mode.
Run StrainScan in low‑coverage mode with the `-l 2` parameter.
Run Strainify on the mixed reads, setting the variant‑filtering window size to 500 bp.
Compute the absolute error between the estimated strain abundances from each tool and the ground‑truth abundance vector.
constraints: ART simulation must use the exact parameter set `-ss MSv3 -p -l 250 -f IC -m 600 -s 150`.
Coverage levels must be 10×, 20×, and 50× for each abundance vector.
StrainScan low‑coverage mode must use the `-l 2` setting.
Strainify variant‑filtering window size must be 500 bp.

instruction: How can antimicrobial susceptibility testing be performed for AMPs against bacterial strains?
steps: Inoculate each bacterial strain onto LB agar plates and incubate at 37 °C for 24 h.
Transfer a single colony from each plate into LB broth and grow with orbital shaking at 150 rpm, 37 °C overnight.
Adjust each bacterial culture to an optical density of OD₆₀₀ = 0.1 and perform a 103‑fold dilution for assay use.
Reconstitute lyophilized AMPs in ultrapure water to a 2.4 mM stock solution.

Dispense 200 µl of reaction mixture into each well of a 96‑well microplate, combining 100 µl LB broth, 95 µl bacterial suspension, and 5 µl of either solvent, spectinomycin (60 µM), or AMP (60 µM) according to the four treatment conditions.
Incubate the microplate at 37 °C for 12 h with shaking as appropriate.
Measure OD₆₀₀ of each well and compute normalized growth using the formula (Test OD−Blank OD)/(Control OD−Blank OD).
Perform each condition in triplicate technical replicates across three independent biological trials.
constraints: Use a 96‑well microplate with 200 µl reaction volume per well.
Maintain incubation temperature at 37 °C for 12 h.
Apply a final AMP concentration of 60 µM in the test group.
Include blank, negative, positive, and test treatment conditions.
Record OD₆₀₀ measurements and calculate normalized growth.
Conduct each experimental condition in triplicate technical replicates and repeat across three independent biological trials.

instruction: How can one process MP‑contaminated soil metagenomic data and build machine‑learning models to predict ARG abundance?
steps: Retrieve 104 metagenomic datasets from CNCB, NCBI, or ENA using Aspera and Kingfisher.
Perform FASTQ quality control and preprocessing with fastp v0.11.8 using a sliding window of 4 bases, Phred score ≥20, maximum N bases ≤5, and minimum sequence length ≥50.
Annotate microbial genera with Kraken2 v2.0.9 and Bracken to obtain genus‑level taxonomic profiles.

Screen potential ARG sequences from quality‑controlled reads and align the filtered FASTA files to the SARG database with ARGs‑OAP v2.3 using alignment length ≥25 aa, similarity threshold ≥80 %, and E‑value cutoff ≤1e‑5.
Normalize genus and ARG abundances to parts per million (ppm) to account for sequencing depth variations.
Apply Pearson correlation analysis to retain microbial genera with |r|≥0.6 and p<0.001 relative to target ARG abundance, then remove genera with inter‑genus |r|≥0.8 to mitigate multicollinearity.
Randomly split the data into an 80 % training set and a 20 % test set, then standardize all features.
Conduct grid‑search hyperparameter tuning combined with 10‑fold cross‑validation for ANN, GBDT, RF, and SVR using the specified number of parameters for each algorithm.
Train the best‑performing model on the full training dataset and evaluate its performance on the test set using R², RMSE, and SMAPE.
Compute SHAP values with the TreeExplainer algorithm (v0.47.0) to quantify feature contributions for each prediction.
Generate partial dependence plots to visualize the marginal effects of key features on predicted ARG abundance.
constraints: Use fastp v0.11.8 with sliding window size 4, Phred score ≥20, max N ≤5, min length ≥50.
Use Kraken2 v2.0.9 and Bracken for genus‑level annotation.
Use ARGs‑OAP v2.3 with alignment length ≥25 aa, similarity ≥80 %, E‑value ≤1e‑5.
Apply Pearson correlation thresholds |r|≥0.6 (p<0.001) for feature selection and |r|≥0.8 for multicollinearity removal.
Divide data into 80 % training and 20 % test sets, then standardize features.
Perform grid search with 10‑fold cross‑validation for ANN (6 params), GBDT (5 params), RF (5 params), and SVR (4 params).
Evaluate models using R², RMSE, and SMAPE.
Use scikit‑learn v1.2.2, xgboost v2.1.4, tensorflow v2.10.0 for model implementation.
Compute SHAP values with TreeExplainer v0.47.0.
Generate partial dependence plots using scikit‑learn.

instruction: How can the main chemical components of white tea be determined by HPLC after extraction?
steps: Weigh 0.2 g of tea powder and transfer it to a container.
Add 5 mL of 70 % methanol aqueous solution to the powder.
Place the mixture in a water bath set to 70 °C and extract for 10 min.
Cool the extract to room temperature.
Centrifuge the cooled extract at 8000 rpm for 5 min.
Collect the supernatant and adjust the volume to 10 mL with the same solvent.
Inject 10 µL of the prepared solution into an LC‑20AT HPLC system.
Set the chromatographic column to ODS‑2 Hypersil (5 µm, 4.6 × 250 mm).
Prepare mobile phase A (0.2 % glacial acetic acid aqueous) and mobile phase B (acetonitrile).
Run the gradient program: 0–4 min 8 % B; 4–32 min 8–30 % B; 32–35 min 30–8 % B; 35–40 min 8 % B.
Maintain column temperature at 28 °C and flow rate at 1 mL min⁻¹.
Detect the eluted compounds at 278 nm.
Quantify each catechin, gallic acid, and caffeine concentration using a standard calibration curve.
constraints: Extraction performed at 70 °C for 10 min with 70 % methanol aqueous solution.
Centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min.
HPLC column: ODS‑2 Hypersil (5 µm, 4.6 × 250 mm).
Mobile phases: A = 0.2 % glacial acetic acid aqueous; B = acetonitrile.
Gradient schedule: 0–4 min 8 % B; 4–32 min 8–30 % B; 32–35 min 30–8 % B; 35–40 min 8 % B.
Flow rate: 1 mL min⁻¹; injection volume: 10 µL; column temperature: 28 °C; detection wavelength: 278 nm.
Quantification based on standard calibration curves for each analyte.

instruction: How can we perform metagenomic sequencing of mouse fecal samples to analyze gut microbiota?
steps: Extract total DNA from each fecal sample using the MagBeads FastDNA Kit for Soil following the manufacturer’s protocols.
Quantify DNA concentration with a Qubit 4 Fluorometer using the dsDNA HS Assay Kit.
Assess DNA integrity by running a 1 % agarose gel electrophoresis with SYBR Safe‑stained gels.
Construct Illumina‑compatible sequencing libraries according to the TruSeq DNA Sample Preparation Guide.
Sequence the libraries on a NovaSeq 6000 platform using paired‑end 2 × 150 bp reads.
Process raw sequencing data with fastp v0.23.2 to remove adapter sequences and low‑quality reads.
Align high‑quality reads to the mouse host genome with minimap2 v2.24‑r1122 and discard reads mapping with a score ≥ 10.
Assemble the remaining reads de novo with MEGAHIT v1.1.2, retaining contigs longer than 300 bp.
Classify contigs taxonomically using Kraken 2 v2.0.8‑beta against a database containing GTDB nucleotide and RVDB viral sequences.
Generate a non‑redundant protein catalog and annotate it with mmseqs2 v13.45111 in search mode.
Quantify gene abundances by re‑aligning reads to the assembled contigs with minimap2 and counting with featureCounts.
Perform functional profiling against KEGG, eggNOG, GO, CAZy, and CARD databases using mmseqs2 to create functional abundance matrices.
Visualize inter‑sample metabolic profile variations with PCA, PLS‑DA, and OPLS‑DA using the R package ropls.
constraints: Use MagBeads FastDNA Kit for Soil for DNA extraction.
Perform sequencing on a NovaSeq 6000 platform.
Use fastp v0.23.2 for quality control of raw reads.
Use minimap2 v2.24‑r1122 for host read removal and read alignment.
Use MEGAHIT v1.1.2 for de novo assembly of filtered reads.
Use Kraken 2 v2.0.8‑beta for taxonomic classification against GTDB and RVDB databases.
Use mmseqs2 v13.45111 in search mode for protein annotation.
Use the R package ropls for PCA, PLS‑DA, and OPLS‑DA analyses.

instruction: How can I characterize the fecal resistome of beef steers across different feeding systems?
steps: Collect approximately 50 g of fecal material per steer and place it in a sterile 24 oz Whirl-Pak bag.
Transport the bags to UCD within two hours and store them at –80 °C until processing.
Thaw the samples at 4 °C overnight before DNA extraction.
Extract total microbial DNA using the DNeasy PowerSoil Pro Kit according to the manufacturer’s protocol.
Measure DNA concentration with a Qubit Fluorometer and assess purity with a NanoDrop spectrophotometer, ensuring 260/280 ratios of 1.8–2.8 and 260/230 ratios >2.
If DNA concentration is below the required threshold, purify the extract with a QIAquick PCR Purification Kit to increase yield.
Send the purified DNA to the UC Davis Genomic Center for library preparation using the Illumina TruSeq DNA kit.
Sequence the prepared libraries on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform with paired‑end 2 × 150 bp reads.
Trim and merge sequencing reads with HTStream, removing reads with a quality score ≤15 and discarding sequences shorter than 36 bp.
Visualize the quality‑filtered reads with MultiQC.
Filter out host DNA by aligning reads to the Bos taurus UMD3.1 reference genome using BWA.
Align the host‑removed reads to the MEGARes 2.0 database with BWA and identify antimicrobial resistance genes using ResistomeAnalyzer.
Assemble reads containing ARGs into contigs with MEGAHIT and align these contigs with BWA‑MEM to determine bacterial origins.
Classify unassembled ARG reads against RUG2 and RefSeq databases using Kaiju.
Align host‑removed reads to Res‑finder (90% identity, 60% minimum length) to detect transferable ARGs.
Align reads to BacMet 2.0 using DIAMOND with an E‑value cutoff of ≤10⁻¹⁰ to identify biocide and metal resistance genes.
Calculate the relative abundance of each ARG and BMRG by dividing gene‑specific read counts by the total reads per sample.
Normalize relative abundances using cumulative sum scaling (CSS) via the metagenomeSeq R package.
Compute Shannon and Chao1 diversity indices with the vegan package to assess alpha diversity.
Generate beta‑diversity ordinations using Non‑Metric Multidimensional Scaling (NMDS) on Bray–Curtis dissimilarities via vegan.
Test for homogeneity of group variances with PERMANOVA (999 permutations) using vegan.
Compute log₂ fold changes between harvest and baseline relative abundances for each ARG class.
Perform two‑way ANOVA with Tukey post‑hoc tests to evaluate feeding system and age effects on diversity metrics.
Conduct pairwise t‑tests on the top 10 ARG classes between groups, adjusting p‑values with the Bonferroni method.
Transform relative abundance data into binary presence/absence matrices for correlation analysis.
Calculate Pearson correlations between ARGs and BMRGs, retaining only correlations with |r| > 0.5 and p < 0.0001.
Visualize alpha diversity, predominant ARG classes, and class comparisons over time using ggplot2.
Plot NMDS ordination results in base R.
Create heatmaps of ARG relative abundance with ComplexHeatmap, normalizing rows by Euclidean distance.
Display transferable ARGs per feeding system with VennDiagram.
Illustrate co‑occurrence networks of ARGs and BMRGs using igraph and ggraph.
constraints: Use the DNeasy PowerSoil Pro Kit for DNA extraction.
Sequence libraries on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 with 2 × 150 bp paired‑end reads.
Trim reads with HTStream and discard those with quality ≤15 or length <36 bp.
Align to the Bos taurus UMD3.1 reference with BWA to remove host DNA.
Detect ARGs with MEGARes 2.0 and classify with ResistomeAnalyzer.
Assemble ARG reads with MEGAHIT and align contigs with BWA‑MEM.
Identify transferable ARGs using Res‑finder with a 90% identity and 60% length threshold.
Identify biocide/metal resistance genes using BacMet 2.0 with DIAMOND E‑value ≤10⁻¹⁰.

instruction: How can microplastic particles be incubated in multiple environmental sites and processed for biofilm analysis?
steps: Collect the five particle types (bio‑beads, polystyrene, HDPE nurdles, wooden beads, glass beads) and sterilise each by autoclaving or gamma‑irradiation as required.
Thread each particle onto a 0.5 mm 316 Grade stainless steel wire, arranging duplicates of each type per wire and spacing them to minimise sunlight bias.
Assemble four biological replicate wires into a structure using crimp beads and enclose the assembly in a sterile box.
Submerge the structure in deionised water, autoclave at 121 °C for 15 min, then remove and air‑dry in a Class‑II cabinet.
Attach the sterilised structure to a weighted rope or cable tie and submerge it in the designated site (hospital wastewater tank, upstream river, downstream dock, marine buoy).
Maintain continuous submersion for a 2‑month incubation period to allow biofilm development.
Retrieve the structure with sterile scissors, place it in a sterile box, and transport it on ice to the laboratory.
Separate the wires from the cable tie using sterile scissors, wash each wire twice with 40 mL sterile 0.85 % NaCl, and air‑dry under sterile conditions.

Cut the particles from the wires with sterile wire cutters and forceps, place duplicate particles into PowerBiofilm Bead Tubes containing lysis buffer, and store the tubes at –70 °C until DNA extraction.
constraints: All handling must occur under sterile conditions (Class‑II cabinet, sterile scissors, forceps, and tubes).
Polystyrene particles must be gamma‑irradiated; all other particles are autoclaved at 121 °C for 15 min.
Structures must be fully submerged with constant exposure and secured to prevent disturbance.
Incubation period must be exactly two months.

instruction: How can I propagate Kyphosus cinerascens gut microbiota in anaerobic microcosms?
steps: Collect macroalgae Turbinaria spp., Dictyota spp., Ulva spp., Halidrys spp., and coralline macroalgae from the specified sites.
Soak and triple wash each macroalgae species in seawater filtered through a 0.22 μm pore size filter to remove sand, epibionts, and macrofauna.
Blend the washed macroalgae and freeze‑dry the mixture into a fine powder.
Collect Kyphosus cinerascens specimens by spear gun at the given coordinates.
Euthanize each fish by pithing following IACUC protocol.
Measure mass and fork length of each fish.
Dissect the fish under anaerobic conditions to retrieve distal hindgut contents.
Combine hindgut contents from six fish to create a single inoculum.
Prepare basal salts medium (BSM) containing MgSO₄, K₂CO₃, CaCO₃, MgCO₃, a 1:10 dilution of artificial sea water, and cysteine hydrochloride monohydrate to render it anaerobic.
Add 4 g L⁻¹ of the freeze‑dried macroalgae powder to the BSM to form the culture medium.
Optionally supplement the medium with type II mucin, bile salts (taurocholic acid and chenodeoxycholic acid), and short‑chain fatty acids as required.
Sieve the combined hindgut inoculum through a sterile 1 mm strainer inside the anaerobic chamber.
Inoculate 50 mL serum bottles (crimp‑sealed with a rubber septum) with the sieved inoculum at a 1:180 dilution ratio.
Flush the 50 mL headspace of each bottle with N₂:CO₂ (80:20) to approximately 29 PSI.
Maintain all cultures at 22 °C throughout the experiment.
At each sampling timepoint, sterilize the bottle opening with an alcohol pad, withdraw 1 mL of culture, mix it with 1 mL of 2X DNA/RNA Shield, and store the mixture at –80 °C.
Aliquot 150 µL of each sample into 96‑well plates for downstream microbiome screening.
constraints: All dissection, inoculation, and sampling must occur within an anaerobic chamber.
The culture temperature must be held constant at 22 °C.
Headspace flushing must use an 80:20 N₂:CO₂ gas mixture at ~29 PSI.
Macroalgae must be added to the medium at a concentration of 4 g L⁻¹.
Inoculum dilution ratio must be 1:180 in 50 mL serum bottles.

instruction: How can we start up and operate an SBR reactor for ultra‑hypersaline wastewater treatment using loose Penicillium steckii MPs and activated sludge?
steps: Prepare washed MPs at a concentration of 1×10⁶ spores L⁻¹.
Add the washed MPs to a 6.5 cm diameter, 2.2 L effective‑volume SBR reactor to achieve an initial mixed‑liquid volatile suspended solids (MLVSS) of 1.31 g L⁻¹.
Add 1 mg VSS of activated sludge to the reactor each day for one week to introduce a diverse microbial community.

Operate the reactor with a 6‑hour cycle consisting of 60 min anaerobic plug‑flow feeding, 281–293 min aeration, 3–15 min settling (decrease to 5 min on Day 2 and to 3 min after Day 7), 2 min discharge, and 2 min idling.
Maintain airflow at 0.5–0.7 cm s⁻¹ and keep the temperature at approximately 21 °C.
Exchange 60 % of the reactor volume each cycle and feed synthetic ultra‑hypersaline wastewater containing 1000 mg L⁻¹ COD.
Monitor the hydraulic retention time to remain at 10 h and adjust loading to achieve an organic loading rate of 2.4 kg COD m⁻³ d.
constraints: SBR diameter: 6.5 cm; effective volume: 2.2 L.
Initial MLVSS: 1.31 g L⁻¹; daily activated sludge addition: 1 mg VSS per day.
Cycle length: 6 h; volume exchange ratio: 60 %.
Anaerobic feeding: 60 min; aeration: 281–293 min; settling: 3–15 min initially, 5 min on Day 2, 3 min after Day 7; discharge: 2 min; idling: 2 min.
Airflow: 0.5–0.7 cm s⁻¹; temperature: ~21 °C.
Influent COD: 1000 mg L⁻¹; hydraulic retention time: 10 h; organic loading rate: 2.4 kg COD m⁻³ d.

instruction: How can I prepare a Pediococcus acidilactici starter culture and ferment manipueira to produce tucupi?
steps: Thaw a frozen aliquot of Pediococcus acidilactici stored at –18 °C and inoculate 10 µL into 10 mL sterile MRS broth.
Incubate the inoculated broth for 24 h in a bacteriological incubator at 35 °C.
Transfer 10 mL of the active culture into an Erlenmeyer flask containing 90 mL sterile MRS broth.
Transfer the inoculum to a benchtop bioreactor containing 900 mL sterile MRS broth.
Maintain agitation at 150 rpm and temperature at 35 °C until the culture reaches 10¹² cells mL⁻¹.
Centrifuge the culture at 10 000 ×g for 14 min at 4 °C and discard the supernatant.
Keep the pellet refrigerated at 4 °C for 24 h before use.
Weigh the pellet and transfer it to the fermentation vessels containing manipueira.
Set up two 8 L polyethylene vessels: one with manipueira only (control) and one inoculated with 1 × 10¹² CFU mL⁻¹ of Pediococcus acidilactici.
Cover the vessels with aluminum foil and incubate them at 30.0 ± 2.0 °C for 24 h.
Collect samples at 0, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, and 24 h for analysis.
After 24 h, heat‑treat the fermented manipueira at approximately 95 °C for 40 min to obtain tucupi.
constraints: Use sterile MRS broth for all inoculations.
Maintain incubation temperature at 35 °C for starter culture growth and 30 °C for fermentation.
Agitate the bioreactor at 150 rpm.
Use benchtop bioreactor with 900 mL volume.
Centrifuge at 10 000 ×g for 14 min at 4 °C.
Add starter culture at a concentration of 1 × 10¹² CFU mL⁻¹.
Incubate fermentation vessels for exactly 24 h.
Heat‑treat at ~95 °C for 40 min.

instruction: How can a laboratory-scale S0PD/A reactor be operated in two phases with defined cycling and inoculation steps?
steps: Assemble a laboratory-scale SBR reactor with a 3.0‑L working volume and set the volume exchange ratio to 50 %.
Install a mechanical stirrer and set it to 150 rpm to mix the reactor contents.
Connect a water bath to recirculate water to the inner and outer walls of the SBR and set the reactor temperature to 35 ± 1 °C.
Operate the reactor in three cycles per day, each cycle lasting 8 h with 7 h of reaction time, 0.5 h of settling time, and 0.5 h of idle time.
During Phase 1 (days 0–70), seed the reactor with secondary‑sedimentation sludge from a wastewater plant and record the initial MLSS at 4.6 g L⁻¹ and MLVSS at 3.7 g L⁻¹.
Add 20 g of S0 powders to the reactor at the start of Phase 1, then add 0.5 g of S0 powders daily according to the NO3⁻‑N loading.
After 70 days, transition to Phase 2 (days 71–170) by introducing 500 mL of anammox granules (MLSS 3.3 g L⁻¹, MLVSS 2.9 g L⁻¹) into the reactor.
Continue operating the reactor with the same cycling regime and daily S0 powder additions throughout Phase 2.
constraints: Reactor working volume must be 3.0 L.
Volume exchange ratio set to 50 %.
Mechanical stirring must be at 150 rpm.
Reactor temperature maintained at 35 ± 1 °C.
Each operational cycle lasts 8 h: 7 h reaction, 0.5 h settling, 0.5 h idle.
Phase 1 lasts 70 days, Phase 2 lasts 100 days.

instruction: How can virome libraries be prepared from lung tissue samples for Illumina sequencing?
steps: Select 37 lung samples for virome analysis by simple random sampling without replacement.
Pool samples into species‑specific groups of five, with separate pools for feral and farmed mink.
Weigh approximately 50 mg of each pooled tissue and mix with 500 µl sterile PBS.
Homogenize the mixture in a mortar.
Centrifuge the homogenate to clarify the supernatant.
Filter the clarified supernatant.
Treat the filtrate with nucleases as described.
Extract total nucleic acids with the QIAamp ® Viral RNA Mini kit.
Amplify the nucleic acids using the WTA2 Complete Whole Transcriptome Amplification kit with protocol modifications.
Clean up post‑amplification DNA by adding 75 µl SPRI magnetic beads (1.5×) to the reaction mixture.
Wash the beads twice with 200 µl 70 % ethanol and air‑dry for 30 s.
Elute DNA with 20 µl nuclease‑free water.
Perform DNA end‑prep by mixing 5 µl of DNA with 0.7 µl Ultra II End‑prep reaction buffer and 0.3 µl Ultra II End‑prep reaction mix and incubating as instructed.

Ligate dual‑index adapters by mixing 6 µl of end‑prep DNA with 3 µl NEBNext Ultra II Ligation Master Mix, 0.1 µl NEBNext Ligation Enhancer, and 0.9 µl 5 µM IDT dual‑index adapters, incubating 15 min at 20 °C, and cleaning up with beads.

Amplify the library by mixing 6.25 µl of post‑ligation DNA with 12.5 µl KAPA HiFi HotStart Ready Mix and 3.1 µl each of 10 µM Illumina p5 and p7 primers, then cycling 95 °C 5 min, 7 cycles of 95 °C 30 s, 65 °C 30 s, 72 °C 2 min, and final extension 70 °C 5 min.
Clean up the amplified library with 0.8× SPRI beads and ethanol.
Verify the library size on a 1.5 % agarose gel.
Submit the library for Illumina NovaSeq 6000 paired‑end sequencing.
constraints: Use QIAamp Viral RNA Mini kit for nucleic acid extraction.
Use WTA2 Complete Whole Transcriptome Amplification kit with protocol modifications from Conciêa‑Neto et al. (2015).
Clean up post‑amplification DNA with 75 µl SPRI magnetic beads (1.5×).
Wash beads twice with 200 µl 70 % ethanol and air‑dry 30 s.
Ligate dual‑index adapters at 20 °C for 15 min using NEBNext Ultra II Ligation Master Mix and 5 µM IDT adapters.
Amplify the library with 7 PCR cycles using KAPA HiFi HotStart Ready Mix and Illumina p5/p7 primers.
Verify the library on a 1.5 % agarose gel before sequencing.

instruction: How can the probiotic suspension be prepared and administered to mice during the ECT intervention period?
steps: Culture Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG and Bifidobacterium longum subsp. longum anaerobically at 37 °C with 5 % CO₂ until the logarithmic growth phase (OD600 = 0.6–0.8).
Collect bacterial pellets by centrifugation at 4000 × g for 10 min.
Wash pellets twice with sterile phosphate‑buffered saline.
Resuspend the washed pellets to a final concentration of 1 × 10⁹ CFU/mL in PBS.
Mix equal volumes of the two resuspended strains immediately before administration.
Prepare a fresh bacterial suspension daily.
Administer 200 µL of the mixed suspension to each mouse by oral gavage between 09:00 and 10:00 a.m. for 10 consecutive days.
constraints: Cultivation must be anaerobic at 37 °C with 5 % CO₂.
Harvest at OD600 = 0.6–0.8.
Resuspension concentration must be 1 × 10⁹ CFU/mL.
Administration volume 200 µL.
Administration timing 09:00–10:00 a.m. for 10 days.

instruction: How can high molecular weight DNA be extracted from sputum samples?
steps: Mix 200 µl of sputum with an equal volume of SPUTOLYSIN® reagent.
Resuspend the mixture with a vortex Genie‑2 mixer and incubate at 37 °C with shaking at 800 rpm for 15 min.
Centrifuge the suspension at 16,060 g for 5 min and discard the supernatant.
Resuspend the pellet in 300 µl sterile 1 × DPBS.
Add an enzymatic mix of 5 µl achromopeptidase (50,000 U ml⁻¹), 1.5 µl lysostaphin (10 mg ml⁻¹), 20 µl lysozyme (50 mg ml⁻¹) and 6 µl mutanolysin (2,000 U ml⁻¹) to the sample.
Incubate the mixture at 37 °C with shaking at 800 rpm for 2 h.
Add 300 µl PowerBead solution to the sample and vortex at maximum speed with a vortex adaptor for 24–1.5 ml tubes.
Extract DNA using the DNeasy Ultra‑Clean Microbial kit following the standard kit protocol.
Quantify the extracted dsDNA with a Qubit 2 fluorometer using the dsDNA high‑sensitivity assay kit.
Optionally, assess DNA size with the Genomic DNA ScreenTape assay on a TapeStation electrophoresis system.
constraints: Use 200 µl of sputum per sample.
Use equal volume of SPUTOLYSIN® reagent.
Incubate at 37 °C, 800 rpm, 15 min for initial lysis.
Centrifuge at 16,060 g for 5 min.
Resuspend pellet in 300 µl sterile 1 × DPBS.
Enzymatic mix amounts: 5 µl achromopeptidase (50,000 U ml⁻¹), 1.5 µl lysostaphin (10 mg ml⁻¹), 20 µl lysozyme (50 mg ml⁻¹), 6 µl mutanolysin (2,000 U ml⁻¹).
Incubate at 37 °C, 800 rpm, 2 h for enzymatic lysis.
Add 300 µl PowerBead solution and vortex at maximum speed with appropriate adaptor.
Follow the DNeasy Ultra‑Clean Microbial kit protocol for DNA extraction.
Quantify dsDNA with Qubit 2 fluorometer and dsDNA high‑sensitivity kit.
Assess DNA size using TapeStation if desired.

instruction: How can metabolomic analysis of plasma samples be performed?
steps: Combine 50 µL of plasma with 450 µL of acetonitrile and mix thoroughly.
Centrifuge the mixture at 13,000 rpm for 15 minutes at 4 °C.
Transfer the resulting supernatant to a clean vial for analysis.
Load the supernatant onto the Dionex UltiMate 3000 UHPLC system equipped with a Thermo Syncronis C18 column (100 mm × 2.1 mm, 1.7 µm).
Prepare mobile phase A as 2 mmol/L ammonium formate with 0.1 % formic acid and mobile phase B as acetonitrile.
Program the gradient: 0–1 min 95 % A, 1–25 min linear to 5 % A, 25–30 min 5 % A.
Set the flow rate to 0.4 mL/min and the injection volume to 3 µL.
Operate the Q‑Exactive mass spectrometer with an ESI source at 2.8 kV, sheath gas 1.05 × 10⁴ mL/min, auxiliary gas 3 × 10³ mL/min, and capillary temperature 320 °C.
Acquire MS1 scans at a resolution of 70,000 across the m/z range 50–1050.
Acquire MS2 data-dependent scans at a resolution of 17,500 using collision energies of 20, 40, and 60 V.
Insert a pooled QC sample after every six test samples to assess instrument stability.
Process raw data with TraceFinder 3.2 for peak detection, alignment, and normalization.
constraints: Use 50 µL plasma and 450 µL acetonitrile for sample preparation.
Centrifuge at 13,000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C.
Use Dionex UltiMate 3000 UHPLC system and Thermo Syncronis C18 column (100 mm × 2.1 mm, 1.7 µm).
Mobile phase A: 2 mmol/L ammonium formate + 0.1 % formic acid; Mobile phase B: acetonitrile.
Gradient: 0–1 min 95 % A, 1–25 min 95 %→5 % A, 25–30 min 5 % A.
Flow rate 0.4 mL/min; injection volume 3 µL.
ESI voltage 2.8 kV; sheath gas 1.05 × 10⁴ mL/min; auxiliary gas 3 × 10³ mL/min; capillary temperature 320 °C.
MS1 resolution 70,000 over m/z 50–1050; MS2 resolution 17,500 with NCE 20, 40, 60 V.
QC sample introduced after every six test samples.
Use TraceFinder 3.2 for data processing.

instruction: How can the effect of B. velezensis A2 combined with BSFL on Salmonella reduction in chicken manure be investigated?
steps: Collect 10 kg of chicken manure from a poultry farm near Huazhong Agricultural University.
Measure the initial Salmonella content of the manure using the PMAxx‑qPCR method.
Culture B. velezensis A2, centrifuge to collect bacterial pellets, and resuspend the pellets in PBS to a concentration of 1 × 10⁹ CFU mL⁻¹.
Place the 10 kg of manure in a plastic container (61 cm × 42 cm × 12 cm).
Prepare three treatment groups: (1) add 100 mL PBS; (2) add 15 000 BSFL and 100 mL PBS; (3) add 15 000 BSFL and 100 mL B. velezensis A2 suspension.
Incubate the containers in a greenhouse set to 28 °C and 65 % relative humidity.
Every 2 days, collect samples of BSFL and frass from each container.
Dry the collected frass in an oven at 105 °C to determine moisture content.
Randomly weigh 100 live BSFL from each container every 2 days.
Determine the Salmonella content in the collected BSFL and frass samples using the PMAxx‑qPCR method.
If Salmonella is below the detection limit, perform enrichment culture and isolation verification according to the Bacteriological Analytical Manual.
constraints: All procedures involving pathogenic bacteria must be performed in a secondary biological safety cabinet.
The greenhouse environment must be maintained at 28 °C temperature and 65 % relative humidity.
Container dimensions must be 61 cm × 42 cm × 12 cm.
BSFL quantity per treatment must be exactly 15 000 individuals.
Bacterial suspension concentration must be 1 × 10⁹ CFU mL⁻¹.
PMAxx‑qPCR must be used for Salmonella detection.
Enrichment culture procedures must follow the Bacteriological Analytical Manual.

instruction: How can I perform in vitro fecal fermentation of the prepared white kidney bean cell samples?
steps: Accurately portion 5 mL of each cell sample, purified protein, CW fraction, and starch into separate serum bottles on a dry‑weight basis.
Add 4 mL of sterile carbonate‑phosphate buffer to each bottle.
Prepare a pooled fecal inoculum by homogenizing donor specimens with buffer at a 1:3 w/v ratio and filtering the mixture through cheesecloth.
Introduce 1 mL of the fecal inoculum into each serum bottle.
Incubate the bottles at 37 °C inside an anaerobic chamber and collect samples at 4, 8, 12, and 24 h.
Measure gas production by penetrating a syringe through the rubber septa of each bottle at the designated time points.
Manually agitate the contents, then centrifuge the supernatants at 12 000 g for 10 min to separate the liquid phase.
Store the resulting supernatants at –80 °C until further analysis.

instruction: How can fungal MAGs be recovered and classified from a cheese metagenomic dataset?
steps: Recover MAGs that passed the prokaryotic contamination and completeness filters of CheckM from the 136 cheese metagenomes.
Use BUSCO v5.3.2 with options --auto-lineage euk --m genome to detect fungal markers in the recovered MAGs.
Select the 158 fungal MAGs that have a BUSCO quality score of at least 50.
Extract 62 fungal core genes from the selected MAGs and reference genomes using UFCG v1.0.6.
Align each core gene with MAFFT v7.487 using the --auto option.
Trim the alignments with trimal v1.4.1 using the -gt 0.1 option.
Concatenate the trimmed alignments and build a maximum likelihood tree with IQ-Tree v2.2.0.3 using options --seqtype AA --m TEST --bb 1000 --alrt 1000, applying ModelFinder for model selection.
Root the tree using Rhizoclosmatium globosum as reference and remove it from the final tree.
constraints: BUSCO v5.3.2 must be run with options --auto-lineage euk --m genome.
Trimal v1.4.1 must use the -gt 0.1 option.
IQ-Tree v2.2.0.3 must be run with options --seqtype AA --m TEST --bb 1000 --alrt 1000 and use ModelFinder.
MAFFT v7.487 must be run with the --auto option.
Fungal MAG selection requires a BUSCO quality score of at least 50.
Only the 62 core genes identified by UFCG v1.0.6 are used for alignment.
Root tree using Rhizoclosmatium globosum as reference and delete it from the final tree.

instruction: How can I conduct a diagnostic accuracy meta‑analysis of mNGS and tNGS studies?
steps: Identify and extract relevant data from each included study, including true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives.
Resolve any discrepancies between extractors by consensus or third‑reviewer adjudication.
Construct a 2×2 contingency table for each study.
Compute sensitivity, specificity, diagnostic odds ratio, and their 95% confidence intervals.
Create forest plots for sensitivity, specificity, and DOR.
Generate HSROC curves using the Reitsma bivariate random‑effects meta‑analysis model.
Calculate the area under the curve (AUC) with 95% confidence intervals.
Visualize results on the SROC plane with confidence and prediction ellipses.
Assess heterogeneity using Cochrane’s Q statistic and I² statistic.
Perform sensitivity and subgroup analyses to explore heterogeneity sources.
For inter‑technique comparisons, compute z‑statistics and p‑values from model‑derived standard errors.
Conduct all statistical analyses in Stata 16.0, R 4.5.0, and RevMan 5.4.0.
constraints: All extracted studies must report true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives.
HSROC modeling must use the Reitsma bivariate random‑effects meta‑analysis framework.
Heterogeneity assessment must employ Cochrane’s Q and I² statistics.
Significance threshold for subgroup analyses is p < 0.05.
Analyses must be performed using Stata 16.0, R 4.5.0, or RevMan 5.4.0.

instruction: How can I conduct an aerobic composting experiment of swine manure and collect samples?
steps: Collect fresh swine manure and sawdust from the National Swine Breeding Engineering Research Center and the Veterinary Teaching Hospital, respectively.
Prepare compost mixtures with carbon-to-nitrogen ratios of 15:1, 25:1, and 35:1 by blending manure and sawdust at mass ratios of 10:1, 4:1, and 2:1.
Adjust the moisture content of each mixture to 60–65% by adding water.
Construct composting reactors measuring 550 mm height × 250 mm length × 250 mm width from steel containers insulated with foam boxes.
Provide aeration to the reactors through timed ventilation.

instruction: How can active atrazine degraders be identified using DNA‑SIP and metagenomics?

Identify OTUs with REF greater than 1 as active atrazine degraders.

instruction: How can the gut virome be profiled from raw metagenomic reads to relative abundance of viral families?

Import the filtered contigs into Geneious Prime for inspection and refinement.
Remove potential vector‑derived contaminants using VecScreen.
Predict open reading frames in Geneious Prime with a minimum length of 100 bp and ATG start codon.

instruction: How can the optimal operational parameters for each physical treatment method be determined using batch experiments?
steps: Collect 30 mL of floated anammox granules from the reactor.
Wash the granules three times with inorganic salt solution (synthetic wastewater without nitrogen sources).
Resuspend the washed granules in 70 mL of the same inorganic salt solution.
Measure settling efficiency as wet weight percentage after 5 min of settling in a graduated cylinder.
For pressure‑transient treatment, place the sludge mixture in a 250 mL vacuum flask connected to a FUJ‑V3 vacuum pump and a digital pressure gauge.
Apply a negative pressure level of –0.02 MPa, –0.04 MPa, or –0.08 MPa.
Measure settling efficiency again after the pressure transient treatment.
For ultrasonic radiation treatment, seal the sludge mixture in a polyethylene bag and submerge it in a KQ5200DB ultrasonic cleaning bath operating at 40 kHz.
Apply a power density of 0.2 W cm⁻², 0.3 W cm⁻², or 0.4 W cm⁻² for 1 min, 5 min, or 10 min.
Measure settling efficiency after the ultrasonic treatment.
For mechanical shaking treatment, place the sludge mixture in a 250 mL sealed, baffled flask and agitate it on a SPH‑2112B thermostatic orbital shaker.
Shake at 50 rpm, 100 rpm, or 150 rpm for 8 h, 12 h, or 24 h.
Measure settling efficiency after the mechanical shaking treatment.
constraints: Use a 250 mL vacuum flask with FUJ‑V3 vacuum pump and digital pressure gauge for PT.
Use ultrasonic bath KQ5200DB with maximum power 200 W at 40 kHz for UR.
Use orbital shaker SPH‑2112B for MS.
Apply negative pressure levels of –0.02 MPa, –0.04 MPa, or –0.08 MPa.
Use ultrasonic power densities of 0.2 W cm⁻², 0.3 W cm⁻², or 0.4 W cm⁻².
Conduct ultrasonic treatments for 1 min, 5 min, or 10 min.
Shake at frequencies of 50 rpm, 100 rpm, or 150 rpm.
Conduct mechanical shaking for 8 h, 12 h, or 24 h.

Measure settling efficiency as wet weight percentage after 5 min of settling.

instruction: How can the effect of P-MEPHC on dry anaerobic digestion of food waste be systematically investigated?

Pre‑activate the inoculum sludge at 35 °C for 48 h before use.
Add the designated amount of P‑MEPHC (13, 26, 39, or 52 mg g⁻¹ VS) to each reactor, with a separate reactor receiving no hydrochar as control.
Seal each reactor and incubate at 30 ± 1 °C in a shaking incubator set to 150 rpm for 30 days.
Conduct the experiment in triplicate for each dosage group to ensure statistical reliability.
Measure daily biogas production and methane content using gas chromatography with a TCD detector.
Monitor liquid‑phase inorganic salts, soluble COD, protein, and polysaccharide concentrations by appropriate spectrophotometric or chromatographic methods.
At the end of the incubation, collect digestate samples for metagenomic sequencing (Illumina NovaSeq) after snap‑freezing and DNA extraction.
constraints: Reactors must have an effective volume of 500 mL and use silicone gaskets for anaerobic sealing.
Substrate and inoculum must be mixed at a 3:1 volume ratio and crushed to ≤2 mm particle size.

P‑MEPHC dosages to be tested are 13, 26, 39, and 52 mg g⁻¹ VS, with a non‑amended control reactor.
Incubation conditions: 30 ± 1 °C, 150 rpm shaking, 30 days duration.
Each experimental condition must be performed in triplicate to allow statistical analysis.

instruction: How can viral RNA be extracted, reverse‑transcribed, and sequenced from pig tissue samples?
steps: Homogenize 0.1 g of spleen, mandibular, or inguinal lymph node tissue with 2 mm stainless steel beads in 1 mL MEM using a TissueLyser II.

Treat the filtrate with 25 U Benzonase, 14 U Turbo DNase I, and 20 U RNase A in a total volume of 150 µL, incubate at 37 °C for 1 h, then at 65 °C for 10 min to inactivate nucleases.
Extract total viral nucleic acids with the QIAamp MinElute Virus Spin Kit following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Quantify the extracted viral RNA with the Equalbit RNA HS Assay Kit.
Reverse‑transcribe the RNA using primer K‑random‑s and Superscript III reverse transcriptase, then synthesize double‑stranded cDNA with Klenow fragment polymerase.
Perform sequence‑independent single‑primer amplification with AccuPrime Taq DNA Polymerase according to the manufacturer’s protocol.
Purify the PCR products using Agencourt AMPure Beads.
Construct cDNA libraries with the VAHTS Universal Plus DNA Library Prep Kit for Illumina.
Validate the libraries on an Agilent 4200 Bioanalyzer and quantify with a Qubit 2.0 fluorometer.
Sequence the libraries on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform.
constraints: Use 0.1 g of tissue per sample.
Use stainless steel beads of 2 mm diameter.
Centrifuge at 8000 ×g for 15 min at 4 °C.

instruction: How can a soil incubation experiment with biochar addition and moisture variability be conducted?
steps: Collect soil from the 0–20 cm layer of a soybean field.
Air‑dry the soil, then remove roots and stones.
Sieve the soil through a 2 mm mesh.
Pre‑incubate 78 g of the sieved soil in culture bottles for 10 days at 25 °C and 60 % WHC, covering bottles with sealing film.
Mix pyrolyzed corn‑straw biochar (500 °C, 1–2 mm) into the soil at 0 %, 1 % or 2 % w/w according to the treatment design.
Transfer the biochar‑mixed soils into PVC‑based ring knives (60 cm³) placed inside the bottles and adjust moisture to the initial maximum WHC.
Maintain the moisture content daily by weighing the bottles 2–3 times per day and replenishing with a dropper as needed to keep comparable average moisture across treatments.
Incubate the soils for 90 days at 25 °C and 40 % relative humidity under a completely randomized 3 × 3 factorial design.

After 90 days, destructively sample the soils and split the material into three parts: (a) air‑dried for physicochemical analysis, (b) frozen at –20 °C for enzyme assays, and (c) frozen at –80 °C for metagenomic sequencing.
constraints: Collect soil from 0–20 cm depth in a soybean field in Fumeng County, China.
Use a 3 × 3 factorial design with biochar addition levels of 0 %, 1 %, and 2 % w/w and moisture regimes of constant 60 % WHC (W0), variable 20–100 % WHC (W1), and variable 40–80 % WHC (W2).
Maintain incubation temperature at 25 °C and relative humidity at 40 %.
Use PVC ring knives with a 60 cm³ volume for the incubation containers.
Maintain soil moisture at the initial maximum water‑holding capacity via daily weighing and dropper replenishment.

instruction: How can the polysaccharides and aqueous extracts of Ficus pumila L. be prepared?
steps: Dry the fruits and leaves at 60 °C for 24 h.
Soak the dried material overnight in 95 % ethanol.
Remove the ethanol and dry the residue at 40 °C.
Boil the material in 10 volumes of water for 2 h, repeat twice, and combine the extracts.
Concentrate the combined extracts under reduced pressure at 60 °C.
Centrifuge the concentrate at 4000 rpm for 10 min.
Precipitate the polysaccharides by adding 95 % ethanol to a final concentration of 80 % and leave undisturbed for 12 h.
Collect the precipitate and treat it with enzymes to remove proteins.
Dialyze the material successively in tap water, distilled water and ultra‑pure water for 24 h each.
Wash the dialyzed material twice with 95 % ethanol.
Freeze‑dry the washed material to obtain the fruit polysaccharide (FPP) and leaf polysaccharide (FPLP).
Cut the fruits into four parts longitudinally and cut the leaves into small pieces.
Grind the powdered material to 40‑mesh particles using a herbal grinder.
Boil the powder first in 10 volumes of water for 2 h, then in 8 volumes of water for 1.5 h.
Combine the two decoctions and filter through a 200‑mesh sieve.
Concentrate the filtrate to a suitable volume and freeze‑dry to obtain the fruit aqueous extract (FPE) and leaf aqueous extract (FPLE).
constraints: Use 95 % ethanol for soaking and precipitation.
Dry at 60 °C for 24 h, then at 40 °C.
Boil in 10 volumes of water for 2 h, repeat, then in 8 volumes of water for 1.5 h for aqueous extracts.
Concentrate under reduced pressure at 60 °C.
Centrifuge at 4000 rpm for 10 min.
Precipitate with ethanol to a final concentration of 80 % and incubate 12 h.
Dialyze successively in tap water, distilled water, ultra‑pure water, each 24 h.
Use 40‑mesh grinding and 200‑mesh sieving.
Freeze‑dry final products.

instruction: How can I isolate, sequence, assemble, and classify microbial genomes from shrimp pond water, sediment, and tissue samples?
steps: Collect 500 ml surface‑water samples at 40 cm depth from four points in sterile plastic bottles and pool them.
Collect 500 g bottom‑sediment samples from the same four points in sterile plastic bags and pool them.
Collect at least 12 shrimp (50–70 g each), dissect Gill, hepatopancreas, and pleopod tissues, pool them, and freeze in a sterile container.
Transport all samples to the laboratory on dry ice.
Store sediment, water, and pooled shrimp tissue samples in a −80 °C freezer until DNA extraction.
Extract genomic DNA from each pooled sample using the Xploregene DNA extraction kit following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Assess DNA purity with NanoDrop spectrophotometry, ensuring A260/A280 ratios between 1.8 and 2.0, and confirm integrity by gel electrophoresis.
Fragment DNA to ~600 bp using the KAPA fragmentation method as part of the KAPA Library Prep Kit protocol.
Perform end repair and A‑tailing with the Hypa Peep Plus ERAT enzyme mix.
Ligate adapters to the repaired fragments using DNA ligase.
Amplify adapter‑ligated DNA with Illumina primers (40 ng input, 10 pM primers) using the PCR cycle parameters: 98 °C 15 s, 60 °C 30 s, 72 °C 30 s for 4 cycles, final extension 72 °C 1 min, hold 4 °C.
Purify amplified libraries with Ampure beads.
Quantify libraries with the Qubit dsDNA High Sensitivity assay kit.
Size‑select fragments >450 bp using a 0.7× bead ratio.
Sequence libraries on the Illumina HiSeq 4000 platform.
Evaluate raw paired‑end read quality with SeqKit, FastQC v0.12.1, and MultiQC v1.17.
Trim adapters and low‑quality bases from reads with Trim_galore v0.6.10 using default parameters.
Align trimmed reads to the human reference genome (hg38.fa) with bowtie2 v2.5.2 and keep unmapped reads.
Align shrimp tissue reads to the Penaeus monodon host genome (NSTDA_Pmon_1) with bowtie2 v2.5.2 and keep unmapped reads.
Assemble contigs from unaligned reads with Megahit v1.2.9 using k‑mer sizes 31 to 141, discarding contigs <200 bp.
Assess assembly quality with QUAST v5.0.2.
Classify assembled sequences taxonomically with Kraken2 against the PlusPF database (Standard plus RefSeq protozoa & fungi, 10/9/2023).
Convert Kraken2 output into an OTU table using the provided Kraken2 script and an in‑house script.
Generate a Krona plot to visualize taxonomic composition.
constraints: Use sterile plastic bottles for water samples and sterile plastic bags for sediment samples.
Transport samples on dry ice and store at −80 °C until DNA extraction.
Follow the Xploregene DNA extraction kit protocol for genomic DNA isolation.
NanoDrop A260/A280 ratio must be between 1.8 and 2.0.
Use the KAPA Library Prep Kit and KAPA fragmentation method for library preparation.
Apply Hypa Peep Plus ERAT enzyme mix for end repair and A‑tailing.
Use DNA ligase for adapter ligation and Illumina primers for amplification.
Purify libraries with Ampure beads and quantify with Qubit dsDNA High Sensitivity assay.
Size‑select >450 bp fragments with a 0.7× bead ratio.
Sequence on Illumina HiSeq 4000.
Trim with Trim_galore v0.6.10 using default parameters.
Align with bowtie2 v2.5.2 to hg38.fa and NSTDA_Pmon_1.
Assemble with Megahit v1.2.9 using k‑mer sizes 31–141, discard <200 bp contigs.
Assess with QUAST v5.0.2.
Classify with Kraken2 against the PlusPF database (Standard plus RefSeq protozoa & fungi).

instruction: How can sequencing data be processed to enable accurate microbial community analysis?
steps: Normalize all raw amplicon sequencing data to an even depth of 6744 reads per sample.
Demultiplex paired‑end and single‑end reads using QIIME2 with the appropriate –type and –input‑format flags.
Trim adapters and primers with the Cutadapt plugin using the primer sequences provided in Table S5.

instruction: How can the aqueous extract of A. membranaceus be prepared and characterized by UPLC‑MS?
steps: Decoct the dried roots of A. membranaceus in purified water using a water‑to‑herb ratio of 8:1 to 10:1 (v/w).
Repeat the decoction three times, each extraction lasting 1.5 h under gentle reflux.
Cool each decoction to room temperature and filter through gauze to remove insoluble residues.
Pool all three filtrates to obtain a combined aqueous extract.
Concentrate the combined extract under reduced pressure to an appropriate volume.
Spray‑dry the concentrated extract to obtain powdered AME.
Dissolve 5.0 mg of AME in 1 mL of 50 % aqueous methanol to prepare a test solution.
Filter the test solution through a 0.22 µm membrane.
Inject 3 µL of the filtered solution into a Waters Acquity UPLC BEH C18 column (2.1 mm × 50 mm, 1.7 µm).
Apply the gradient mobile phase: 0–20 min 5 %–40 % acetonitrile (A); 20–30 min 40 %–100 % A; 30–35 min 5 % A.
Set the flow rate to 0.3 mL/min and maintain the column temperature at 30 °C.
Detect the eluent with an AB SCIEX TripleTOF 6600 mass spectrometer using an ESI ion source in both positive and negative modes, scanning m/z 50–1500.
Set the ion source temperature to 350 °C and apply ionization voltage of 4 kV, capillary voltage of 35 V, and tube lens voltage of 110 V.
Use high‑purity nitrogen (99.99 %) as sheath and auxiliary gas with flow rates of 40 arb and 20 arb, respectively.
For quantitative analysis, use an AB SCIEX API 4000 QTRAP in multiple reaction monitoring mode with transitions 447.1→285.1 (C7G), 431.1→269.1 (ononin), and 785.5→143.1 (ASIV).
Optimize declustering potential and collision energy for each analyte to achieve maximum signal intensity.
Apply a 5‑minute gradient on an ACQUITY UPLC BEH C18 column: 0–0.5 min 5 %–70 % B, 0.5–1 min 70 %–95 % B, hold 95 % B for 2 min, and equilibrate at 5 % B for 2 min.
Record the total analysis time as 5 min.
constraints: Use purified water for extraction.
Maintain a water‑to‑herb ratio of 8:1 to 10:1 (v/w).
Perform three 1.5‑hour extractions with gentle reflux.
Filter decoctions through gauze before pooling.
Concentrate extracts under reduced pressure.
Spray‑dry the concentrated extract to powder.
Prepare test solution by dissolving 5.0 mg AME in 1 mL 50 % aqueous methanol.
Filter test solution through a 0.22 µm membrane.
Use a Waters Acquity UPLC BEH C18 column (2.1 mm × 50 mm, 1.7 µm).
Set flow rate to 0.3 mL/min and column temperature to 30 °C.
Detect with AB SCIEX TripleTOF 6600 using ESI ion source, scanning m/z 50–1500.
Use high‑purity nitrogen (99.99 %) as sheath and auxiliary gas.

instruction: How can we process sediment metagenomic samples to assemble MAGs and quantify gene abundances?
steps: Extract DNA from sediment samples using the FastDNA Spin Kit for Soil following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Perform paired‑end shotgun metagenomic sequencing (PE150) on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform.
Filter raw reads for quality and remove adapters and primer sequences using Trimmomatic v0.39 with LEADING:3, TRAILING:3, SLIDINGWINDOW:4:20, and MINLEN:36.
Assemble clean reads into scaffolds using SPAdes v3.15.5 with –meta and k‑mer sizes 21, 33, 55, and 77.
Call protein‑coding sequences on scaffolds longer than 500 bp using Prodigal v2.6.3 with –p meta.
Cluster called CDS at 95 % identity using CD‑HIT v4.8.1 with -c 0.95 and -aS 0.9 to create a non‑redundant gene set.
Quantify CDS abundance with Salmon v1.9 in mapping‑based mode (-validateMappings -meta) and normalize read counts to genes per million reads (GPM).
Derive metagenome‑assembled genomes (MAGs) from assemblies (minimum length 1000 bp) using MetaBAT 2, MaxBin 2, CONCOCT, SemiBin v2.1.0, and VAMB v4.1.3.
Integrate and refine MAGs from all binning tools using the Bin_refinement module of metaWRAP v1.3.2 with -c 50 and -x 10.
Assess completeness and contamination of refined MAGs with CheckM v1.0.13.
Dereplicate MAGs at the species level using dRep v3.4.0 with -comp 50, -con 10, and an ANI cutoff of 95 % to obtain a representative genome set.
Calculate relative abundance of each dereplicated MAG using CoverM v0.6.1 with parameters –trim-min 0.10, –trim-max 0.90, –min-read-percent-id 0.95, and –min-read-aligned-percent 0.75.
constraints: Sediment samples must be stored at -80°C before DNA extraction.
DNA extraction must be performed with the FastDNA Spin Kit for Soil following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Sequencing must be performed on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform using PE150 chemistry.
Quality filtering of raw reads must use Trimmomatic v0.39 with LEADING:3, TRAILING:3, SLIDINGWINDOW:4:20, and MINLEN:36.
Assembly must use SPAdes v3.15.5 with –meta and k‑mer sizes 21, 33, 55, and 77.
Gene prediction must use Prodigal v2.6.3 with –p meta.
CDS clustering must use CD‑HIT v4.8.1 with -c 0.95 and -aS 0.9.
Abundance quantification must use Salmon v1.9 in mapping‑based mode (-validateMappings -meta).
Binning must use MetaBAT 2, MaxBin 2, CONCOCT, SemiBin v2.1.0, and VAMB v4.1.3.
Refinement of bins must use metaWRAP v1.3.2 with –c 50 and –x 10.
Completeness and contamination assessment must use CheckM v1.0.13.
Dereplication must use dRep v3.4.0 with -comp 50, -con 10, and an ANI cutoff of 95 %.
Relative abundance calculation must use CoverM v0.6.1 with –trim-min 0.10, –trim-max 0.90, –min-read-percent-id 0.95, and –min-read-aligned-percent 0.75.

instruction: How can model cheese be manufactured under controlled conditions?
steps: Heat treat whole cow’s milk at 68 °C for 10 minutes.
Cool the milk to 33 °C.
Divide the cooled milk into five 10‑L containers.
Add 0.02 g / 100 mL food‑grade CaCl₂ to each container.
Add the mixed starter culture of Lactococcus lactis subsp. lactis and Lactococcus lactis subsp. cremoris.
Add liquid calf rennet at 1.8 mL per 10 L of milk (V/V).
Cut the curd 60 minutes after renneting.
Heat the curd to 37 °C with gentle stirring and rest for 10 minutes.
Drain the whey and incubate the coagulum at 38 °C until the pH reaches 6.2 (≈30 minutes).
Add 3 % (m/m) salt to the coagulum.
Pour the salted curd into cylindrical moulds.
Press the moulds at room temperature until the pH reaches 5.3.
Vacuum‑pack each cheese weighing 50 g.
Ripen the cheeses at 8 °C and 16 °C for 120 days.
Sample the cheeses every 30 days.
constraints: Maintain a constant salt concentration of 3 % (m/m) in the curd.
Use ripening temperatures of exactly 8 °C and 16 °C.

Use liquid calf rennet at 1.8 mL per 10 L of milk (V/V).

instruction: How can I determine the coumarin and AHL contents in A. dahurica roots and surrounding soil using UPLC‑QqQ/MS?
steps: Weigh 1.0 g of dried A. dahurica root powder.
Add 34 mL of 87% methanol to achieve a solid‑to‑liquid ratio of 1:34.
Sonicate the mixture at 40 kHz, 25 °C for 15 min.
Centrifuge the extract at 12 000×g for 10 min and collect the supernatant.
Filter the supernatant through a 0.22 µm membrane filter.
Dilute the filtered extract to fall within the linear calibration range.
Place 10 g of rhizosphere soil into a conical flask and add 50 mL ethyl acetate.
Shake the flask at 180 rpm for 2 h.
Allow the mixture to stand for 30 min and then centrifuge at 5 000×g for 10 min.
Collect the ethyl acetate extract and repeat the extraction step two more times.
Combine the ethyl acetate extracts and evaporate the solvent completely.
Add 1 mL of 20% methanol to the residue and sonicate at 40 kHz for 20 min.
Filter the resulting solution through a 0.22 µm membrane filter.
Inject both the root and soil extracts into an Agilent 1290 Infinity II UHPLC system coupled to a 6475 Triple‑Quadrupole mass spectrometer.
Perform MRM analysis using the external standard method to quantify coumarins and AHLs.
constraints: Use 87% methanol for root extraction at a solid‑to‑liquid ratio of 1:34.
Sonicate at 40 kHz, 25 °C for 15 min for roots and 20 min for soil extracts.
Centrifuge root extracts at 12 000×g for 10 min, soil extracts at 5 000×g for 10 min.
Filter all supernatants through a 0.22 µm membrane.
Shake soil–ethyl acetate mixture at 180 rpm for 2 h.
Use an Agilent 1290 Infinity II UHPLC system with a 6475 Triple‑Quadrupole MS and electrospray ionization.

Reassemble the consolidated bins using the metaWRAP reassembly_bins module to improve N50 values and reduce contamination
Evaluate the resulting bins with CheckM2 v1.1.0 to estimate genome completeness and contamination

instruction: How can we induce and assess acute protein energy malnutrition in mice?
steps: Purchase C57Bl6/J mice and house them under specific‑pathogen free or gnotobiotic conditions.
Assign mice to either a control diet (20% protein) or a reduced‑protein diet (0.7% for adults, 2% for young mice) ensuring the diets are isocaloric.
Feed the assigned diet for four weeks while maintaining a 12‑hour light cycle and providing gamma‑irradiated chow ad libitum.
Weigh each mouse weekly and collect fecal pellets immediately on dry ice, then store them at –80 °C.
At the end of the four‑week period, euthanize one half of each diet group by cervical dislocation.
Collect blood from the vena cava of the euthanized mice.
Harvest tissues of interest for subsequent histological and molecular analyses.
constraints: Use C57Bl6/J mice aged 10–16 weeks for adults and 4 weeks for young mice.
Diet compositions must be 20 %, 0.7 %, or 2 % protein as specified and isocaloric.
Mice must be housed under specific‑pathogen free conditions or in gnotobiotic isolators.
Maintain a 12‑hour light/dark cycle.
Provide gamma‑irradiated chow diet ad libitum.
Collect fecal pellets on dry ice and store at –80 °C.
Blood must be collected from the vena cava before euthanasia.
Euthanasia performed by cervical dislocation.

instruction: How can the virome of Tibetan pig fecal samples be prepared and sequenced using pooled libraries?
steps: Collect approximately 100 µL of supernatant from each fecal sample.
Pool the samples into 38 pools, adjusting the volume per sample to 100 µL for 5-sample pools, ~85 µL for 6-sample pools, and ~125 µL for 4-sample pools.
Centrifuge each pooled sample at 13,000 g for 5 min at 4 °C.
Pass the resulting supernatant through a 0.45 µm filter to remove eukaryotic cells and enrich viral particles.
Treat the filtered supernatant with DNase and RNase at 37 °C for 60 min to digest unprotected nucleic acids.
Extract total nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) using the QiAamp Viral RNA Minikit following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Reverse‑transcribe the extracted nucleic acids with SuperScript IV and 100 pmol random hexamer primers to generate cDNA.
Perform single‑round DNA synthesis using Klenow fragment polymerase.
Construct 38 libraries with the Nextera XT DNA Sample Preparation Kit according to the kit protocol.
Sequence the libraries on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform using 150 bp paired‑end reads with dual barcoding.
constraints: All processing must occur in a biosafety level 2 laboratory.
Disposable gloves and sterile containers must be used for sample collection.
Samples must be transported on dry ice and stored at –80 °C within 48 h of collection.
Pipette tips and consumables must be RNase‑ and DNase‑free and equipped with aerosol filters.
Centrifugation of pools must be performed at 13,000 g for 5 min at 4 °C.
DNase and RNase digestion must be conducted at 37 °C for 60 min.
Filtering through a 0.45 µm filter must precede nucleic acid extraction.

Assess DNA quality using agarose gel electrophoresis and quantify DNA with a Qubit®2.0 Flurometer.
Generate paired‑end sequencing libraries and sequence them on the Illumina NovaSeq PE150 platform at a depth of 20 G.
Use Trimmomatic (v.2.39) to remove adapters and primers and to discard reads shorter than 50 bp or with Phred score below 20.
Assemble the filtered reads into contigs using Megahit (version 1.1.2) with a co‑assembly strategy, a minimum contig length of 500 bp, k‑step = 10, and k‑min = 27.
