Assemble filtered reads with metaSPAdes v3.15.2 using default parameters.
Estimate contig abundances by mapping reads back to assemblies with CoverM v0.7.0 using the –methods rpkm option.
Assign taxonomy to reads and contigs with Kraken2 v2.0.8‑beta against the modified PlusPF database.

Detect plasmid contigs with Platon v1.6 and assess lateral gene transfer events with Waafle software.

Identify mobile genetic elements with MobileOG‑db v1.6, predict genes with Prodigal v2.6.3, and align to MobileOG with DIAMOND v2.1.12 using e‑value ≤1e‑20, % identity ≥90%, coverage ≥90%, and length ≥100 aa.
Infer gene‑level abundances of ARGs and MGE genes using the count‑per‑million (CPM) normalization method.
constraints: Use a 3M Environmental Scrub Sampler Stick with 10 mL Wide Spectrum Neutralizer for all sampling.
Homogenize samples with a stomacher at 175 rpm for 2 min in 10 mL sterile PBS.
Centrifuge at 5 000 g for 5 min at room temperature; store pellets at –80 °C.
Extract DNA with the DNeasy PowerSoil Pro kit and quantify with a Qubit fluorometer.
Prepare libraries with the NGS DNA Library Prep Set and sequence on Illumina NovaSeq 6000 150 bp paired‑end.
Trim reads with TrimGalore v0.6.0 using –paired –nextera –stringency 5 –length 75 –quality 20 –max n 2 –trim‑n.
Remove host reads with Bowtie2 v2.3.4.3 –sensitive‑local against phage phiX174, human GRCh38, Sus scrofa.
Assemble with metaSPAdes v3.15.2 default.
Map reads to contigs with CoverM v0.7.0 using –methods rpkm.
Taxonomy assignment with Kraken2 v2.0.8‑beta against the modified PlusPF database.
ARG annotation with Bowtie2 v2.4.2 and BLASTn v2.12.0 against ResFinder.
Plasmid detection with Platon v1.6 and LGT assessment with Waafle.
MGE detection with MobileOG‑db v1.6, gene prediction with Prodigal v2.6.3, alignment with DIAMOND v2.1.12 using e‑value ≤1e‑20, % identity ≥90%, coverage ≥90%, length ≥100 aa.
Consider ARGs within 5 kb of an MGE on the same contig as potentially mobile.
Use CPM normalization for gene abundances.

instruction: How can lettuce heads and adjacent soil be sampled and preserved for microbiome sequencing?
steps: Randomly select lettuce heads along each row of the field.
Cut each selected head off with a knife while wearing gloves.
Clean the knife with 70 % ethanol and change gloves before harvesting a different cultivar.
Remove outer leaves from each head and place half of the head separately into a bag.
Collect approximately 100 g of soil with a sterile spoon 1 cm below the surface at three random locations adjacent to the selected lettuce head.
Combine the three soil subsamples to form a composite sample for each cultivar.
Place each lettuce head portion and each composite soil sample in separate coolers with cooling packs immediately after sampling.
Ship the coolers overnight to the laboratory.
Store the soil composite samples at –80 °C upon arrival at the lab.
constraints: Sampling must occur at full head maturity.
Use 70 % ethanol to clean knives.
Use sterile spoons for soil sampling.
Transport samples in coolers with cooling packs.
Store soil composite samples at –80 °C.

instruction: How can the ProFiT‑SPEci‑FISH pipeline be performed to explore plasmid dynamics and determine bacterial host in environmental samples?
steps: Sequence the environmental sample using high‑throughput sequencing.

Run ProFiT on the assembled contigs to generate digoxigenin‑labeled probe sequences targeting the plasmids of interest.
Order the produced probes.
Fix and permeabilize bacterial cells extracted from the environmental sample.
Hybridize the ordered probes to the fixed cells following the SPEci‑FISH protocol.
Wash away unbound probes.
Incubate the cells with anti‑digoxigenin antibodies conjugated to horseradish peroxidase.
Add tyramide‑labeled fluorophores to catalyze signal amplification.
Visualize the fluorescent signal using a fluorescence microscope or analyze the labeled cells with fluorescence‑activated cell sorting (FACS).
constraints: Sequencing depth must be sufficient to assemble plasmids with copy numbers as low as ~5 copies per cell.
ProFiT probe sets must minimize cross‑reactivity with non‑plasmid DNA and cover target plasmids entirely.

instruction: How can soil samples be processed for lignocellulose analysis?
steps: Oven‑dry the soil samples at 40 °C for 24 h.
Weigh 0.5 g of the oven‑dried soil into an Ankom F57 fibre filter bag and heat‑seal the bag.
Perform sequential NDF and ADF procedures on the filter bag using an Ankom 2000 analyser following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Wash the filter bag in acetone for 5 min and oven‑dry it at 105 °C for 4 h, then weigh the bag.
Conduct acid detergent lignin (ADL) extraction in a daisyII incubator for 3 h following the manufacturer’s instructions, then air‑dry the samples.
Oven‑dry the ADL‑treated samples at 105 °C for 4 h and weigh them.
Determine the ash content by combusting the samples in a Carbolite CWF 1200 muffle furnace at 525 °C for 3 h, then weigh the ash residue.
Calculate the proportions of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin from the measured values.
constraints: Oven‑drying temperature must be 40 °C for 24 h.
Use an Ankom F57 fibre filter bag for 0.5 g soil samples.
NDF and ADF procedures must be performed on an Ankom 2000 analyser following manufacturer instructions.

Oven‑drying after acetone wash must be at 105 °C for 4 h.
ADL extraction must be conducted in a daisyII incubator for 3 h following manufacturer instructions.
Ash determination must use a Carbolite CWF 1200 muffle furnace at 525 °C for 3 h.

instruction: How can hyperthermophilic composting be performed using a semi‑automated intelligent aerobic composting device?

Periodically collect compost samples for hydrocarbon analysis at predetermined time points.

instruction: How can PM2.5 and TSP concentrations be determined from quartz filter samples collected in a broiler house?
steps: Place the quartz filter membrane (diameter 90 mm) into the ambient air particulate matter sampler (Model: Laoying 2030) positioned at a height of 1 m in the middle of the chicken house.
Operate the sampler at a working flow of 100 L min⁻¹ for 48 h to collect PM2.5 and TSP.
After sampling, remove the quartz filter and place a portion of the film under controlled constant temperature and humidity for 24 h to equilibrate moisture.
Weigh the equilibrated quartz film and record the mass as W1.
Calculate the mass of collected PM2.5 or TSP as (W1 − W0), where W0 is the initial dry weight of the filter before sampling.
Compute the concentration of PM2.5 or TSP by dividing the mass difference by the total sampling volume (sampling flow × sampling time).
constraints: Use ambient air particulate matter sampler (Model: Laoying 2030).
Quartz filter membrane diameter must be 90 mm.
Sampling flow must be 100 L min⁻¹ for 48 h.
Equilibrate filter at constant temperature and humidity for 24 h.
Weigh filter with precision sufficient to detect mass differences.
Concentration calculation uses formula (W1 − W0)/(sampling flow × sampling time).

instruction: How can high-resolution mass spectrometry be performed to identify HPyV6 proteins in tumor and adjacent healthy tissues?
steps: Extract total protein from 40 mg of tissue using cold RIPA lysis buffer with protease inhibitor cocktail and 5 mM EDTA, homogenizing with gentleMACS C tubes and dissociators.
Centrifuge the homogenate at 4000 g for 5 min at 4 °C and collect the supernatant containing soluble proteins.
Load 50 µg of the protein sample onto a 4–15 % Mini‑PROTEAN TGX Stain‑free gel and run SDS‑PAGE.
Transfer proteins from the gel onto a nitrocellulose membrane using a Mini Trans‑Blot Electrophoretic Transfer Cell.
Block the membrane in blocking solution and incubate overnight at 4 °C with the patient’s serum diluted 1:1000.
Wash the membrane and incubate 1 h at room temperature with biotin‑conjugated mouse anti‑human IgM/IgG (1:1000).
Incubate 30 min at room temperature with HRP‑conjugated streptavidin (1:2000).

instruction: How can we extract DNA from stool samples and generate metagenomic sequencing libraries for analysis?

Transfer the mixture to the PowerLyzer Fastprep tube and bead‑beat on Fastprep for one cycle of 45 s at 4.0 m/s.
Centrifuge the lysate at 10 000 × g for 30 s and transfer the supernatant to a clean 2 ml Collection Tube.
Add 250 µl Solution C2, vortex for 5 s, and incubate 5 min at 2–8 °C.

Centrifuge at maximum speed 30 min at room temperature, discard supernatant, wash the pellet with 400 µl of 70 % ethanol, and centrifuge again 5 min.

instruction: How can we process fecal metagenomic sequencing data to generate a fungal abundance profile?
steps: Run fastp v0.20 to perform quality control on raw reads, trimming polyG tails and removing reads shorter than 90 bp, with mean Phred score below 20, more than 30% of bases with Phred score below 20, sequence complexity below 30%, or unpaired reads.

Compute transcript‑per‑million (TPM) values by dividing each genome’s normalized count by the total normalized counts in the sample and multiplying by one million.
Sum the TPM values of all genomes assigned to a given taxon to obtain taxonomic abundances.

Map the PacBio reads to the grapevine reference genome VHP‑T2T.hap1 and discard host‑derived sequences.
Pool all unmapped reads from the sampled accessions into a single dataset.
Assemble the pooled reads into contigs with assemblers such as Flye, hifiasm, hifiasm‑meta, metaFlye, metaMDBG, or Verkko.
Bin the assembled contigs into draft genomes using CONCOCT, Maxbin 2.0, and MetaBAT 2.
Dereplicate the bins with Galah to eliminate redundant genomes.
Select the non‑redundant MAGs that satisfy the medium‑quality threshold defined by standard MAG quality assessment criteria.
constraints: Sequencing must be performed on the PacBio Sequel IIe platform.
Host filtering requires mapping to the VHP‑T2T.hap1 grapevine genome.
Assembly can use any of the following tools: Flye, hifiasm, hifiasm‑meta, metaFlye, metaMDBG, Verkko.
Binning must employ CONCOCT, Maxbin 2.0, and MetaBAT 2.
Dereplication must be done with Galah.
Selected MAGs must meet the medium‑quality criteria according to standard MAG quality assessment.

instruction: How can untargeted metabolomic profiling of ruminal fluid samples be performed?
steps: Thaw ruminal fluid samples at 4 °C.
Aliquot 100 µL of each thawed sample into a microcentrifuge tube.
Add 500 µL of pre‑cooled methanol/acetonitrile (1:1 v/v) to each aliquot.
Vortex the mixture thoroughly.
Centrifuge at 12,000 × g, 4 °C, for 15 min.
Collect the liquid phase and dry the supernatant in a vacuum concentrator.
Dissolve the dried residue in aqueous acetonitrile (1:1, v/v).
Vortex and centrifuge again at 12,000 × g, 4 °C to collect the final supernatant.
Inject the prepared sample into the Acquity UPLC HSS T3 column (1.8 µm, 2.1 × 100 mm) on the Waters Xevo G2‑XS Q‑TOF mass spectrometer.
Acquire LC‑MS data in electrospray ionization dual‑polarity mode with both low and high collision energies.
Include a quality‑control composite sample and a blank to monitor system stability.

Apply electrospray ionization in dual‑polarity mode.
Operate LC‑MS with low and high collision energies.
Use a pre‑cooled methanol/acetonitrile (1:1 v/v) extraction solvent.
Centrifuge at 12,000 × g for 15 min at 4 °C.
Dry extracts in a vacuum concentrator.
Re‑dissolve in aqueous acetonitrile (1:1, v/v) before injection.
Employ an Acquity UPLC HSS T3 column (1.8 µm, 2.1 × 100 mm).

Harvest cells by centrifugation, wash twice with 20 mM Tris‑HCl buffer (pH 7.4), and resuspend in the same buffer.

Analyze eluted fractions by SDS‑PAGE on a 12 % gel to assess purity.

Determine protein concentration using the Bradford assay.

Aspirate the supernatant through a 0.22‑µm polypropylene syringe membrane filter to remove sludge.

instruction: How can plasma metabolite profiles be determined and analyzed?

Reconstitute the dried extract in 2 % acetonitrile.
Inject the reconstituted sample into an UHPLC system (Agilent 1290) coupled with a TripleTOF 5600 mass spectrometer.

Generate a data matrix of m/z, retention time, and peak areas.
Identify metabolites by comparing spectra with the Metabolites Database.
Apply PLS‑DA to assess metabolite significance and select metabolites with VIP ≥ 1.
Perform pathway analysis using MetaboAnalyst 6.0.
Interpret the resulting metabolic profiles.

steps: Shred low‑grade tobacco leaves.

Spray the enzyme and yeast suspension uniformly onto the shredded leaves.
Place the sprayed leaves in a climate‑controlled incubation chamber set to 37 °C and 50 % relative humidity.

Fabricate each sample into cigarettes.
Ensure batch homogeneity by thorough mixing and sampling from randomized locations within the chamber.
Conduct sensory evaluation of the finished cigarettes by a panel of assessors in a controlled room at 30 °C and 70 % relative humidity.
Score eight attributes on a 9‑point scale.
Calculate the overall sensory score as the unweighted sum of the attribute scores.
Determine the optimal fermentation time based on the highest overall score.

instruction: How can we identify high-confidence viral contigs from metagenomic sequencing data?

Assemble the retained reads de novo with MEGAHIT v1.1.1 to produce contigs of at least 2 kb in length.
Predict viral sequences from the assembled contigs with DeepVirFinder v1.0 using a score threshold of ≥0.7 and a p‑value threshold of ≤0.05.
Cross‑validate the predicted viral contigs with VirSorter2 v2.2.4, retaining only those with a confidence score of ≥0.95.
Assess the quality and completeness of the remaining contigs with CheckV v1.0.3, applying thresholds of ≥95% nucleotide identity, ≥85% alignment fraction, and coverage of the shortest contig.
Keep only contigs that contain at least two viral hallmark genes.
Extract proviral regions from the retained viral genomes using CheckV’s contamination estimates.

instruction: How can we perform a harmonized amplicon-metagenomic meta-analysis of TB sputum microbiome data?

Filter out samples with fewer than 500 reads and ASVs present in a single sample.
Exclude taxa reported as common laboratory contaminants (blocklist).
Implement decontam with a prevalence threshold of 0.1 using the lowest‑read samples as pseudo‑controls.
Merge data using the phyloseq R package and analyze diversity with the microbiome package.
Visualize data with ggplot2, test associations with PERMANOVA, and identify differentially abundant taxa with DESeq2.

instruction: How can I perform a metabolomic analysis of tobacco shoots?
steps: Freeze‑dry tobacco shoots using an Alpha 1–4 LSC.
Grind the freeze‑dried samples with a Tissue Lyser II.
Extract the powder twice with methanol at 1.5 mL per 100 mg.
Dry the extracts and dissolve them at 10 g L⁻¹ in methanol.
Inject 2 µL of each extract into an Accurate‑Mass Q‑TOF 6546 LC‑MS system.
Separate compounds at 40 °C on a 120 EC‑C18 column (3.0 × 100 mm × 2.7 µm) using the specified gradient.
Acquire mass spectra in positive ion mode with nitrogen nebulization at 12 L min⁻¹, 40 PSG pressure, capillary voltage 3500 V.
Explore chromatograms with Mass Hunter Qualitative Analysis software.
Process the data with the Galaxy platform Workflow4metabolomics.
constraints: Use an Accurate‑Mass Q‑TOF 6546 coupled with an LC 1290 Infinity II system.
Separation performed at 40 °C on a 120 EC‑C18 column (3.0 × 100 mm × 2.7 µm) with 0.4 % formic acid in water as eluent A and acetonitrile as eluent B.
Gradient: 0.7 mL min⁻¹, 1 % to 57 % B in 8.5 min, 57 % to 93 % B in 7 min, 93 % to 100 % B plateau 16.5–18.5 min.
Mass analysis in positive mode with nitrogen nebulization at 12 L min⁻¹, 40 PSG pressure, capillary voltage 3500 V.

instruction: How can the colonization of polyethylene terephthalate, polyethylene, and polypropylene microplastics in wastewater treatment units and their subsequent biological characterization be carried out?
steps: Select PET, PE, and PP microplastics as research objects.
Cut the microplastics into the same size of 4.87 ±0.53 mm in length and 115.83 ±3.97 μm in diameter using disinfected scissors.
Characterize the physico‑chemical properties of each microplastic type.
Fill fine mesh bags with 100 microplastic items per liter of each type.
Place each mesh bag inside a stainless steel cage (12 cm diameter, 10 cm depth, 0.8 mm mesh size).
Arrange three cages at each of the five process units (Influent-I, Anaerobic-A1, Anoxic-A2, Aerobic-O, MBR-M).
Fix the cages in place with chains and ropes to prevent displacement by sewage flow.
Maintain the cages for 60 days to allow colonization.
Collect the colonized microplastic samples (MPMI) and natural sewage water (NSW).
Wash each MPMI three times with sterile water in sterile dishes at room temperature for 45 minutes.
Air‑dry the washed samples for 45 minutes.
Stain the dried samples with a 1 % crystal violet solution (1 % w/v) for 45 minutes.
Discard the surplus staining solution and transfer the samples to a new sterile culture dish.
Air‑dry the dyed samples for 45 minutes.
Transfer each sample to a 2 mL centrifuge tube containing 1.5 mL of 95 % (v/v) ethanol and decolorize for 10 minutes.
Transfer the resulting solution to a microcolourimetric dish.
Measure the absorbance at 595 nm using a UV‑1800 spectrophotometer with 95 % (v/v) ethanol as blank.
constraints: Use PET, PE, and PP microplastics.
Cut to 4.87 ±0.53 mm length and 115.83 ±3.97 μm diameter.
Use disinfected scissors.
Use fine mesh bags with 100 items/L.
Use stainless steel cages of 12 cm diameter, 10 cm depth, 0.8 mm mesh size.
Maintain cages for exactly 60 days.
Stain with 1 % crystal violet (1 % w/v) for 45 minutes.
Decolorize with 95 % (v/v) ethanol for 10 minutes.
Measure absorbance at 595 nm with UV‑1800 spectrophotometer.

instruction: How can I generate relative functional abundance profiles from oral rinse whole metagenome shotgun sequencing data?
steps: Collect oral rinse samples from participants.
Perform whole metagenome shotgun sequencing on Illumina NovaSeq 6000 and NovaSeq X platforms with 150 bp paired-end reads.
Demultiplex raw sequencing reads using bcl-convert v3.8.2.
Quality filter the reads with fastp v0.23.1 using parameters –e 25 and –l 70 to remove low quality and short reads.
Align the quality-filtered reads to the human reference genome GRCh38.p14 with BWA-MEM v0.7.17 using default settings to remove host DNA contamination.
Retain paired-end reads where neither mate mapped to the human genome as microbial reads.
Annotate the microbial reads with mi-faser v1.61 against the Fusion database to obtain function read counts.
Annotate the microbial reads with HUMAnN3 v3.9 by aligning to species pangenomes and the UniRef90 database (version 201901b) to generate gene family abundance tables.
Convert the gene family tables to EC numbers using the humann_regroup_table utility.
Remove functions with read counts less than five from both Fusion and EC profiles.
Normalize the remaining function counts by the total number of microbial reads per sample to produce relative abundance profiles.
constraints: Sequencing must use Illumina NovaSeq 6000 and NovaSeq X platforms with 150 bp paired-end reads.
Demultiplexing must be performed with bcl-convert v3.8.2.
Quality filtering must employ fastp v0.23.1 with –e 25 and –l 70.
Host removal must use BWA-MEM v0.7.17 aligned to GRCh38.p14 with default parameters.
Functional annotation must use mi-faser v1.61 with the Fusion database containing 433,891 functions.
Functional annotation must also use HUMAnN3 v3.9 with species pangenomes and UniRef90 v201901b.
Functions with read counts below five must be excluded before normalization.
